The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
Candidate: Anant Shri
Abstract
This thesis investigates mirror symmetry in three-dimensional quantum field theories
with reduced supersymmetry, focusing on theories with N ≤2. While mirror symmetry
for N = 4 theories admits a clear geometric interpretation in terms of Type IIB brane
constructions and SL(2; Z) duality, this intuition breaks down once supersymmetry is
reduced since the moduli space may be lifted, and canonical brane realizations are often
unavailable.
Despite the breakdown of the geometric intuition afforded to us by N = 4 supersymmetry,
we show that mirror-like dualities persist beyond the conventional N = 4 setting. Guided
by symmetry considerations – most notably the exchange of flavor and topological sym-
metries – we develop a systematic framework to construct mirror duals of non-Abelian
N = 2 Chern-Simons-matter theories. We analyze unitary and symplectic gauge groups,
study their renormalization group flows under real mass deformations, and map gauge-
invariant operators across dual descriptions. The framework further admits extensions
to non-supersymmetric theories, revealing a broader notion of three-dimensional mirror
symmetry as a duality between order and disorder operators.

Acknowledgements
I find it hard to believe how quickly the last four years have gone by. When I came to
Trieste in September 2022, I could not have imagined how incredible this phase of my life
would be, and this is in no small part due to the amazing people I have met during the
course of my studies. What started as a joke - “the real String Theory was the friends we
made along the way” - feels very true today. It has been a privilege getting to know and
spending the last few years with all of you. I will carry you all wherever I go. This has
been an absolute joy!
Sergio Benvenuti.
First and foremost, I would like to thank my supervisor for his
constant support and motivation. His creativity and intuition for the subject are mind-
blowing.
I learned a great deal about this subject through working with him, and I
recognize how fortunate I have been to have a supervisor like him.
Sara Pasquetti, and Riccardo Comi. I am very grateful for our continued collabora-
tions. I have learned a lot working with you.
The professors at SISSA. I would be remiss if I did not thank them for designing and
teaching the advanced courses that laid the foundation for this work, especially Aleksandr
Azatov, Giulio Bonelli, Andrea Romanino, Roberto Percacci (who very kindly gave me a
copy of his book), Laura Donnay, and Alberto Lerda.
Charanjit S. Aulakh. I thank my former supervisor for his insight, help, and invaluable
support.
My family. I would like to thank my mother, father, grandmother, aunt, uncle, Ishan
and Pranav, Sujata didi, and my dog, Troy the Sausage. This work would not have been
possible without their constant love and support.
Simone Rota. This thesis would be incomplete without mentioning you. It has been a
joy to discuss physics (and life) with you, and I am deeply grateful for our friendship. At
some point, I will successfully gaslight you into going to Bosnia with me and/or joining a
communist party with me.
Gabriel Pedde Ungureanu. I cannot reflect on the last four years of my life without
mentioning you. I am grateful for our friendship, and I have learned so much from you. I
wish you the best, and I cannot wait to see all the amazing things you do in your life.
Vittorio Cagioni.
It has been a pleasure to work with you last year.
I have really
enjoyed doing physics with you (thank you for always going along with whatever crazy
quivers I showed you), and I cannot wait to see all the great things you do in the next few
years. You are a brilliant physicist, and I wish you the best.
vii

Alessandro. It is difficult for me to imagine the last few years without you. Thank you
for everything (especially for introducing me to halloweenmath, I am very grateful). We
still need to see Godzilla vs. Kong! However, I concede and officially forego the desire to
undertake more suicidal trips.
Quoc-Trung, Mohamed Khalil, and Ideal. Thank you for being my office mates and
for surviving the terror of my Nerf gun (no small feat).
Amartya, and Marco Venuti. The PhD would not have been the same without both
of you. Thank you for surviving the PhD along with me, and I am truly grateful for our
friendship. Amartya - you are one of the best flatmates I could have ever asked for, and I
am so thankful. I wish nothing but the best for you and Ekta.
Marina (the Demolator), Alberto, and our Lady and Savior, Keiko the cat. The
last three years would not have been the same without you. I will never be able to express
how much I value the time we have spent together, and I am profoundly grateful for
everything.
Ben, Shrey, Vincenzo, and Francesco Gentile. It is always a pleasure spending time
with you guys. Thank you for putting up with my incessant stream of Bollywood brainrot.
I promise we make good movies too, none of which I will show you.
Master Michele Fossati. You have unleashed a monster by introducing me to Italian
brainrot, and you can be sure that you will be plagued by Tralalero Tralala for the rest of
time.
Giulio Neri.
I find myself at a loss for words when I try to express how much our
friendship has meant to me. I cherish your presence and all the moments we have spent
with each other (including the amusement park in Vienna). I am very grateful for your
love and support. Thank you for everything, and I send you (and Moon) love.
Francesco Rinaldi. The man, the myth, the legend. Your presence makes everything
better (even Napoli), and I am profoundly grateful for your warmth and kindness. I do
look forward to watching Jawan with you sooner than later. I also apologize if I skewed
the dataset for your experiments.
Kendall. It has been lovely spending time with you. Your enthusiasm and ability to find
joy are contagious. Friendsgiving and Halloween occupy a special place in my heart.
Francescopaolo. I am deeply grateful for your presence in my life. I truly value our
long walks discussing life and science. Such a lovely man can only be accompanied by an
equally wonderful woman, and it has been a joy spending time with you and Annalaura.
Vi voglio bene!
Leonardo. The only reason the non-supersymmetric project came about was that I knew
how much seeing a quiver in three dimensions would annoy you. In all honesty, I cannot
imagine the last few years without you, and I extend my gratitude to you for not having
reported me to the Questura for all the times I did not join you for drinks. I am very
grateful for your presence in my life, and it is hard for me to picture my life without you.
Lorenzo and Chiara. It has been a pleasure spending time with both of you. I always
look forward to it, and to cooking more Indian food for you. I promise we make good
movies (RRR is not a good movie), and I might show you some of them. I send both of
you love.
Val. SISSA (and the trip to Sofia) would not be the same without your kindness, warmth,
viii

and mindful presence. I will always be grateful for whatever time we spend together -
though we could spend more if the meditation group timings were shifted by an hour.
Alfredo and Freya. It is very hard for me to imagine the last few years without the
two of you. Your kindness, warmth, friendship, and presence mean more to me than I can
express. Trieste feels empty now that you are not here. Freya - at some point, we will
start a bakery that will drive Mapo out of business so we can take over their spot. Alfredo
- we need to go to a Dua Lipa concert while my permesso di soggiorno is valid.
La Nostra Gloriosa Regina Diletta. Your warmth and kindness make Italy a much
better place. I am profoundly grateful and will conquer a neighboring country for you
during the next Barcolana. Le mie scarpe sono elettriche!
Emma Icardi.
I am so glad I went to the meditation group session once, because I
would never have met you otherwise! I am so thankful for your kindness, compassion,
and presence! I cannot imagine the last few months without you, and I am profoundly
grateful.
Friends in Trieste. I cannot forget Dr. Marco Detoni (my therapist, who also knows
about planar Abelianization now), Francesco Verdiani, Mak (and Haso), Reza, Claudio
(the freshest man in Europe), Carlos, Francesco Benetti, Florie, Harman, Gayathri G.,
Anindita, Claudia, Clemente, Cecilia, Lodovico, Maria Vittoria, Valentina, Minahil, il
Nostro Signore Marco Pasini, Elvira, Ayush, Aliza, Giorgos, Francesco Gabrielli, Up-
manyu, Farid, Idris, Tomás, Tomasso, Michele Tortora, Johann, Stefano, Fabrizio, Pietro,
Paul, Lucas, Marco Caramanti, Gorka, Simone Corbo, Thomas, and Massimo, all of whom
made my life in Trieste much better.
Other cool people I meet every 6 months at conferences.
Thank you Chiara
Mascherpa, Andrea Zanetti, and Pietro Glorioso, for making the Spring Schools at ICTP
more fun. It has also been a pleasure to interact with Noppadol, Simone Giacomelli, and
Antonio Amariti.
Davide Morgante. A special thanks to you — without your memes, I would not have
survived the last three months of coursework (or the rest of my PhD here). All the TikZ
diagrams in this thesis owe their existence to you. Additionally, you helped me make my
CV and LinkedIn profile, and all future employers will also extend their thanks to you for
the same (for real, thank you for enduring my rants about late-stage capitalism).
Baso (l’uomo più bello della galassia). Your presence in my life means so much to
me. I am deeply grateful. I send you lots of love and cannot wait to see the amazing things
you do in your life. I also look forward to starting our boat fishing cartel in Amsterdam.
Maël (Jean-Claude). The best Frenchman in the world. I always look forward to seeing
you. For you, I would consider coming to France again. Baguette Baguette!
Anna Antonini. You are such a lovely and amazing person. I am so glad we have kept
in touch, and I am very grateful for your presence in my life. However, I am going to win
the postcard game, just saying.
The Student Secretariat at SISSA. Thank you Evy, Marco, and Riccardo — you are
lifesavers, and I am grateful for your help navigating the tenth circle of inferno that is
Italian bureaucracy through these years.
The Mensa Staff at SISSA. Thank you so much Patrizia, Barbara, Debora, and Lucio
for your warmth and kindness!

Aditi (Peanut). Your presence makes Europe a much warmer place. I am always grate-
ful for the time we spend together (and always look forward to our biannual European
adventures).
The very lovely and wonderful Mahak. I am so very grateful for the time we spend
together (it is always a source of joy for me). Words will never be enough for when I try
to express the importance you hold in my life. I am deeply grateful for your presence, and
I always send you love. Whatever you do, wherever you go, I know you will be amazing,
and I hope you remember that you will always have a place in my heart.
Srijita (Gully Girl).
I would not have survived my PhD without your kindness and
patience. I am so grateful for your presence in my life, and I will never be able to completely
express how much your thoughtfulness and compassion mean to me.
Vaishnav. Thank you for deterring me from applying for another PhD by reminding me
of the coursework in the first year. Please give me a job once you finish your PhD. That
is the only thing keeping me going. Thanks.
Vedanth. I am profoundly grateful for your presence in my life. I value the time we
spend together, and I am very grateful for your love and support. Such a wonderful man
can only be accompanied by the equally amazing Anoushka, and I send both of you love
and wish you the best.
Sumedha. I am very glad we have stayed in touch all these years. I always look forward
to hearing from you and am grateful for your presence.
Urvashi (Whitey). I am so glad that we have stayed in each other’s lives. I am so grateful
for your presence and for your postcards. I send you and Ajay love, and hope this is the
year you get a Schengen visa and visit me (because I sure as hell am not getting a British
visa).
Prajwal. Thank you for all your warmth and support over these years. Italy has been
such a wonderful experience, in no small part due to your presence in Padova. I am so
very grateful for our friendship. I am also grateful for our Spotify blend, which always
introduces me to new Punjabi hits (which is surprising given that neither of us knows
enough Punjabi to warrant this).
Shesha (Godfather Gopal). I always look forward to our annual meetups. It is lovely
spending time with you.
Isha. It would be an understatement to say that this thesis (and my PhD by extension)
owes its existence to you. Your kindness and warmth mean the world to me, and I would
never have made it as far as I have without your presence in my life. I wish you all the
happiness in the world.
Friends from India. Thank you for staying in touch with me over the years. I cherish the
time we spend together and send you love: Navneet, Nilanjana, Gayathri, Annesh, Kham-
lek, Varun, Vishal, Taniya, Surbhi, Nirmitee, Pavan, Adarsh, Anjira, Kasturi, Spandan,
Deeksha, Disha, Sachin, Avni, Dhanashree, Aditya, Bhuvana, Gaurav, Rudresh, Annirudh,
Simli, Mewan, Srikrishnaa, Adrija, Meghna, Raunak, Sai, Sanghita, Estuti, and Arijeet.
I am also profoundly grateful to Madhura, Riya, Rhea (and the equally lovely Minu),
Roshni, Mehr, Navya, Shantanu, Ahmed, Rehmat, Ojassvi, Mehar, Zainab, and Sumer.
I send all of you all the love in my heart; I could never have imagined having such a
large family in all corners of the world. All of you have been the best of me, and I am

so grateful. This work would not have been possible without all of you. Thank you for
everything. I hope we remain in each other’s lives in the years to come.

Publications
This thesis includes work by the author that has been published or accepted for publica-
tion. These publications are the own work of the author of this thesis, and the author
has the permission of the publishers to reproduce the contents of these publications for
academic purposes.
In particular, some data, ideas, opinions and figures presented in this thesis have pre-
viously appeared or may appear shortly after the submission of this thesis as follows:
1. S. Benvenuti, R. Comi, S. Pasquetti, G. Pedde Ungureanu, S. Rota, A. Shri, “Planar
Abelian Mirror Duals of N = 2 SQCD3”, in Phys. Rev. D 112.10 (2025) [2].
2. S. Benvenuti, R. Comi, S. Pasquetti, G. Pedde Ungureanu, S. Rota, A. Shri “A
Chiral-Planar Dualization Algorithm for 3d N = 2 Chern-Simons-Matter Theories”,
in JHEP (10) (2025) [3].
3. S. Benvenuti, R. Comi, S. Pasquetti, G. Pedde Ungureanu, S. Rota, A. Shri “Planar
Abelian Duals of Chern-Simons QCD”, in JHEP (05) (2026) [4].
4. S. Benvenuti, R. Comi, G. Pedde Ungureanu, S. Rota, A. Shri “Universal Planar
Abelian Duals for 3d N = 2 Unitary CS-SQCD”, arXiv: 2603.08842 [5].
5. S. Benvenuti, V. Cagioni, S. Rota, A. Shri “Universal Planar Abelian Duals for 3d
N = 2 Symplectic CS-SQCD”, arXiv: 2605.06776 [6].
Except where reference is made in the text of the thesis, this thesis contains no other
material published elsewhere or extracted in whole or in part from a thesis accepted for
the award of any other degree or diploma. No other person’s work has been used without
due acknowledgment in the main text of the thesis. This thesis has not been submitted
for the award of any degree or diploma in any other tertiary institution.

Captatio Benevolentiæ
Motivation
A key feature at the heart of our understanding of supersymmetric gauge theories is the
idea of universality [7–13]: two vastly different theories may furnish the same macroscopic
description upon flowing to the same infrared fixed point (IRFP) [14–18]. Consequently,
when the renormalization group (RG) flow renders one theory non-perturbative due to
strong coupling, one may still extract useful information about the system by studying a
more perturbatively amenable dual theory.
With the advent of the Second Superstring Revolution, this notion of universality has
become pervasive in contemporary physics. Embedding a quantum field theory in String
Theory [19] sheds light on properties of the former that would not be immediately visible
otherwise; chief among these are various infrared dualities which are more easily uncovered
by exploiting the geometric properties of brane setups.
Nowhere is this perspective more striking than in three-dimensional supersymmetric gauge
theories, which often admit two very distinct kinds of dual descriptions. In addition to
the more familiar Aharony-like dualities [18, 20, 21] which retain the global symmetry
structure, 3d gauge theories also display mirror symmetry [22] which exchanges topological
and flavor symmetries.
3d N = 4 mirror symmetry can be immediately understood in terms of Type IIB Hanany-
Witten brane setups [19] as a consequence of the S-duality of the latter (inherited from the
SL(2; Z) invariance of IIB String Theory), which exchanges NS5 and D5 branes. Thus,
3d N = 4 mirror symmetry relates pairs of theories with the classically exact Higgs and
quantum-corrected Coulomb branches of the moduli space exchanged.
It was believed that a large amount of supersymmetry is necessary to observe this kind of
IR duality. While generalizations to 3d N = 2 Abelian theories were possible [23–25], their
non-Abelian counterparts proved to be far more elusive and were only recently discovered
by invoking generalized bifundamentals as building blocks [26, 27].
N ≤2 theories have far richer dynamics than their N = 4 counterparts since they admit
the inclusion of Chern-Simons (CS) terms [28–31]. The subject of mirror symmetry in
the context of N ≤2 Abelian CS theories was first studied in [24, 25, 32, 33] & [34–44]
respectively, but generalizations to non-Abelian N ≤2 theories are not as straightforward
and merit renewed attention.
Rather than viewing reduced supersymmetry as a limitation, we treat it as an opportu-
nity: Chern–Simons interactions and chiral matter introduce new infrared behaviors that
are invisible in N = 4 settings, but can still be organized into precise duality frameworks.
Motivated by this, we study the infrared dynamics of 3d N ≤2 unitary and symplectic
xvii

gauge theories with CS interactions. The central theme of this thesis is that mirror sym-
metry, even in the absence of strong geometric control, can be systematically engineered
using symmetry principles and real-mass flows, yielding large families of dualities across
supersymmetric and non-supersymmetric regimes.
Organizational Note
The thesis is organized into five parts, each addressing a distinct aspect of mirror symme-
try in three-dimensional quantum field theories, while building progressively toward less
supersymmetric and more general settings.
1. Part I: Introduction
This part provides the theoretical background and sets the stage for the rest of the
thesis. We begin with a concise review of supersymmetric quantum field theories,
with particular emphasis on 2 + 1-dimensional dynamics. We then introduce brane
constructions in Type IIB string theory, focusing on their role in the geometric engi-
neering of three-dimensional gauge theories. The part concludes with a pedagogical
review of 3d N = 4 mirror symmetry, which serves as the conceptual and technical
anchor for the subsequent developments.
2. Part II: N = 2 Mirror Symmetry in Unitary Gauge Theories
In this part, we develop systematic methods for constructing mirror duals of 3d
N = 2 unitary Chern-Simons-matter gauge theories. We introduce an algorithmic
procedure based on real mass deformations to generate large classes of N = 2 dual
pairs. Particular attention is devoted to the renormalization group flows, the map-
ping of global and topological symmetries, and the identification of gauge-invariant
operators across the duality. This part incorporates the main results of the published
works on unitary theories [2, 3], as well as their extensions to massive deformations
[5].
3. Part III: N = 2 Mirror Symmetry in Symplectic Gauge Theories
This part extends the techniques developed for unitary gauge groups to symplectic
gauge theories. We construct mirror duals of 3d N = 2 theories with symplectic
gauge symmetry, highlighting both the similarities and the new features that arise
relative to the unitary case. Renormalization group flows and massive deformations
are analyzed in detail, providing further evidence for the proposed dualities. This
part incorporates the results of [6].
4. Part IV: Non-Supersymmetric Planar Mirror Symmetry
In this part, we explore extensions of the mirror symmetry framework to non-
supersymmetric quantum field theories.
Guided by insights from the supersym-
metric constructions, we propose planar Abelian dual descriptions for classes of
non-supersymmetric Chern-Simons-matter theories. We discuss which structural as-
pects of mirror symmetry persist in the absence of supersymmetry, which features
are modified, and which remain conjectural, thereby delineating the scope and lim-
itations of these non-supersymmetric dualities. This part is based on the results of
[4].
5. Part V: Conclusions and Outlook
The thesis concludes with a summary of the main results and a discussion of open
xviii

problems and future directions. We outline possible generalizations of the dualiza-
tion algorithms developed in this work, including the implementation of an SL(2, Z)-
based construction for N = 2 theories, extensions to orthogonal and orthosymplectic
gauge groups, and connections to two-dimensional N = (2, 2) theories. These direc-
tions highlight the broader applicability of the methods introduced in this thesis and
point toward a more unified understanding of infrared dualities in low-dimensional
quantum field theories.

List of Figures
Action of S-duality on a Type IIB Hanany–Witten configuration. The left
panel shows the original setup realizing N = 4 U(N) SQCD with F hyper-
multiplets: N D3-branes are suspended between two NS5-branes, with F
D5-branes providing the flavors of the theory. Applying the S element of
SL(2; Z) exchanges NS5- and D5-branes, yielding the configuration shown
in the middle panel.
This intermediate configuration violates the s-rule
and therefore does not preserve supersymmetry. To restore supersymme-
try, one must perform a sequence of Hanany–Witten moves, during which
D3-branes are created or annihilated as fivebranes cross. The resulting su-
persymmetric configuration, shown in the right panel, realizes the mirror
dual theory. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

between U(N) SQCD with [F, 0] fundamental fields and a planar, Abelian
quiver gauge theory obtained by adding a suitably chosen real mass defor-
mation to N = 4 U(N) SQCD with [F, F] flavors and its mirror dual (top
row). The electric N = 2 non-Abelian SQCD has CS level (−F
2 + N, −F
2 )
and a vanishing superpotential, whereas the mirror N = 2 Abelian, pla-
nar quiver has CS and mixed CS interactions, a “planar” superpotential
inherited from the N = 4 parent theory, and a monopole superpotential
generated via the Polyakov Mechanism [190] (captured by Wmirror). Flavor
and topological symmetries are exchanged by the duality, such that disorder
operators (monopoles) are mapped to more familiar “mesonic” operators.

2.10 Applying the N = 4 dualization to U(N) SQCD with [F + 2N, F + 2N]
flavors proceeds as follows - in the first line, the quiver is broken into its
constituent QFT blocks and each block is sandwiched between an S−1 and
S-wall. We have explicitly kept the integral and integrating measures to re-
mind the reader that care must be taken when the gauge nodes are ungauged
to implement the algorithm. The resulting QFT blocks obtained after the
local action are written in the second line, and are glued back together by
exploiting the fusion to identity property of the S-wall to obtain the final
theory in the third line. At this stage, an explicit Lagrangian description
may be written using the UV completion of the FT[U(N)] theory, which is
indeed the mirror description one would get by utilizing conventional HW
brane setups. Note that we would need to incorporate HW transitions to
dualize more general quiver theories. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Part I: Introduction

Chapter 1
Geometric Engineering of
Three-Dimensional
Supersymmetric Gauge Theories
Supersymmetry has played a central role in shaping our modern understanding of quan-
tum field theory beyond perturbation theory. By severely constraining the structure of
quantum corrections, supersymmetric theories admit exact results that would otherwise
be inaccessible, allowing for the controlled study of strongly coupled dynamics. Among
the most striking consequences of these constraints is the existence of nonperturbative
dualities: distinct microscopic descriptions that flow to the same infrared fixed point.
String theory provides a natural framework in which many such dualities acquire a geomet-
ric origin. In particular, extended objects such as D-branes furnish concrete realizations
of supersymmetric gauge theories, with their low-energy dynamics captured by world-
volume field theories. Operations that are highly nontrivial from a purely field-theoretic
perspective—such as strong–weak coupling dualities, symmetry enhancements, or infrared
equivalences—often translate into simple geometric manipulations of brane configurations.
Three-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories offer a particularly rich arena for these
ideas. Unlike their four-dimensional counterparts, they exhibit intrinsically nonperturba-
tive operators, such as monopoles, which play a central role in determining the infrared
dynamics. At the same time, three-dimensional theories admit a wide class of dualities
whose origin is most transparent when viewed through their string-theoretic embeddings.
Chief among these is mirror symmetry, an infrared duality that exchanges the Higgs and
Coulomb branches of N = 4 theories and interchanges perturbative and nonperturbative
degrees of freedom.
In this chapter, we review the interplay between supersymmetry, brane constructions, and
mirror symmetry in three dimensions. We begin with a brief overview of supersymmetry
and D-branes and their low-energy worldvolume descriptions, emphasizing the emergence
of supersymmetric gauge theories from string theory. We then introduce the Hanany–
Witten construction, which provides a geometric realization of three-dimensional gauge
dynamics and their dualities. Within this framework, mirror symmetry arises naturally as
the action of Type IIB S-duality, offering a clear geometric interpretation of the exchange
between Coulomb and Higgs branch physics.
This review serves two purposes. First, it establishes the standard framework for mirror

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
symmetry in N = 4 theories, which will be used extensively throughout this thesis. Second,
it motivates the central question addressed in later chapters: how much of this structure
survives when supersymmetry is reduced, brane realizations are no longer available, and
the classical notions of Higgs and Coulomb branches cease to exist. The techniques and
perspectives introduced here will provide the foundation for extending mirror symmetry
beyond its traditional setting.
The View From Field Theory
Supersymmetry provides a powerful theoretical framework in which strongly coupled quan-
tum field theories can be studied with a remarkable degree of analytical control. In three
spacetime dimensions, supersymmetric gauge theories exhibit a rich interplay between
gauge dynamics, global symmetries, and quantum effects that has led to deep insights into
infrared physics, including the existence of nontrivial fixed points and intricate patterns
of duality. The purpose of this section is to review the aspects of supersymmetric field
theories that will be essential throughout this thesis, with an emphasis on structures that
are robust under renormalization group flow and therefore characterize the infrared theory.
To that end, we mainly collect and review some key results from [45–56].
Rather than aiming for a comprehensive introduction to supersymmetry, we focus on the
ingredients that play a central role in our study of three-dimensional dynamics. This ma-
terial will serve both to fix conventions and to establish a common language for discussing
infrared dualities, mass deformations, and symmetry realizations in later chapters.
In
this sense, the section is intended as a conceptual and technical foundation, highlighting
those features of supersymmetric gauge theories that persist beyond a specific ultraviolet
description and govern the universal infrared behavior.
A Prelude: Supersymmetry in Diverse Dimensions
The history of supersymmetry is rather peculiar, even when compared to that of most
other topics in theoretical physics. Originally proposed in the early 1970s, supersymmetry
was progressively developed into a remarkably rich and elegant mathematical framework.
It unifies particles of different spins into common symmetry multiplets and has far-reaching
implications for both quantum field theory and fundamental physics [52].
Early attempts to extend the symmetry structure of relativistic quantum field theories were
motivated by the idea of combining spacetime symmetries with internal gauge symmetries.
In particular, it appeared natural to seek a unified framework incorporating the Poincaré
group in four dimensions, ISO(1, 3), together with the gauge group of the Standard Model,
SU(3)c × SU(2)L × U(1)Y . Concrete proposals along these lines were explored, including
attempts to embed these symmetries into a larger group such as SU(6) [52]. However,
these ideas were ultimately ruled out by a series of no-go theorems, the most notable of
which is the Coleman–Mandula theorem:
Theorem 1 (Coleman–Mandula Theorem) The most general bosonic symmetry of
scattering amplitudes is a direct product of the Poincaré group and an internal symmetry
[57]
G = ISO(1, 3) × Ginternal .
In other words, under the assumptions of the theorem, there exists no non-trivial way of
combining spacetime and internal symmetries within a purely bosonic framework.
However, like all good no-go theorems, the Coleman–Mandula result admits a crucial loop-

Chapter 1
hole: it assumes that all symmetry generators are bosonic. Shortly after its formulation,
this limitation was exploited with the emergence of supersymmetry. In 1975, Haag, Ło-
puszański, and Sohnius extended the theorem to include fermionic symmetry generators,
thereby identifying supersymmetry as the most general consistent extension of spacetime
symmetries:
Theorem 2 (Haag–Łopuszański–Sohnius Theorem) The most general symmetry of
the S-matrix consists of the Poincaré group, internal symmetries, and additional fermionic
generators that form a non-trivial extension of the Poincaré algebra [58].
The resulting super-Poincaré algebra is a Z2-graded1 algebra [60–62]: its even part co-
incides with the ordinary Poincaré algebra, while its odd part is generated by spino-
rial charges. At a physical level, supersymmetry transformations exchange bosonic and
fermionic degrees of freedom, mapping particles of integer spin to particles of half-integer
spin and vice versa.
In this sense, supersymmetry constitutes a natural and minimal
extension of the known symmetries of relativistic quantum field theory.
Remarkably, supersymmetry is the only known symmetry that can relate bosons and
fermions in a quantum-consistent way. More generally, it is the only symmetry of the
S-matrix capable of connecting states with different spin-statistics. Classical symmetries
relating fields of different spin are typically spoiled by quantum effects, unless they are
protected by supersymmetry. As a consequence, sufficiently extended supersymmetry is
often required for classical geometric and algebraic structures of field theory to survive
quantization and retain a precise mathematical meaning [49].
The exact form of the Poincaré algebra depends on the zoology of spinors in various
dimensions and the corresponding space-time signatures. However, the general structure is
fairly universal, and we comment on that before focusing on supersymmetry in 3 spacetime
dimensions.
Spinors in D Dimensions
A spinor is an element of a complex vector space S on which the universal Dirac matrices
Γµ act. In D spacetime dimensions, the complex dimension of S is
dimC S = 2⌊D/2⌋.
The space S furnishes a representation of the complexified spin group Spin(D; C).
In even dimensions, D ≡0 (mod 2), the spinor representation is reducible and decomposes
into two irreducible chiral representations,
S = S+ ⊕S−,
corresponding to positive and negative chirality. Elements of S± are called Weyl spinors.
The properties of spinors depend crucially on the spacetime signature (p, q). In particular,
the spinor space S may admit a real, complex, or quaternionic structure compatible with

{., .} : a ⊗a →a[−n] satisfying graded antisymmetry and graded Jacobi relations [59]

Supercharges and Superalgebras
To make manifest the automorphism group of the supersymmetry algebra, it is convenient
to write the supercharges in terms of minimal spinors. When the supersymmetry auto-
morphism is a physical symmetry of the theory, we refer to it as the R-symmetry of the
theory.
In supersymmetric theories, N denotes the number of independent supersymmetry gen-
erators, counted in units of the minimal spinor appropriate to the spacetime dimension
and signature. Equivalently, N measures how many copies of the minimal supersymmetry
algebra are realized in the theory. Since the dimension and reality properties of spinors

Chapter 1
depend on the spacetime dimension, a fixed value of N corresponds to a different total
number of real supercharges in different dimensions. In even dimensions, where chiral
spinors exist, supersymmetry may further decompose into independent left- and right-
moving sectors, characterized by a pair (NR, NL). Table 1.3 summarizes the values of
N (or (NR, NL)) that are physically allowed in various spacetime dimensions, subject to
spinorial constraints and consistency of the supersymmetry algebra.

Chapter 1
Aspects of Supersymmetry in Three Dimensions
The results presented in this thesis are rooted in the study of three-dimensional gauge
theories. Having established the general structure of the supersymmetry algebra, we now
turn to a review of several features that are specific to supersymmetric field theories in
three spacetime dimensions.
These theories exhibit a number of distinctive properties
— both technical and conceptual — that set them apart from their four-dimensional
counterparts and make them particularly rich and tractable objects of study3.
Elements of N = 4 and N = 2 Gauge Theories
The Supersymmetry Algebra.
The supersymmetry algebra of (2 + 1)-dimensional
N = 4 theories is built upon the bosonic Poincaré algebra

Representation Theory of the Supersymmetry Algebra.
The elementary repre-
sentations of the N = 4 supersymmetry algebra are the vector multiplet and the hyper-
multiplet. Since the theories studied in this thesis preserve at most N = 2 supersymmetry,
it is convenient to express these multiplets in terms of N = 2 representations.
The basic building blocks of a three-dimensional N = 2 theory are6
chiral multiplet:
Q = (φ, ψα, F) ,
vector multiplet:
V = (Aµ, λα, σ, D) .
Here φ and F are complex scalars, ψα and λα are two-component fermions, and σ and
D are real scalars. The auxiliary fields F and D enter algebraically and determine the
moduli space of supersymmetric vacua through their equations of motion.
In terms of N = 2 multiplets, an N = 4 theory decomposes as
hypermultiplet:
H = QN=2 ⊕˜QN=2 ,
vector multiplet:
V = VN=2 ⊕ΦN=2 ,
where Q and ˜Q transform in conjugate representations R and ¯R, and Φ is an adjoint chiral
multiplet.
6A useful intuition is to view 3d N = 2 theories as arising from dimensional reduction of 4d N = 1
theories [47, 48].

A 0-form Topological Symmetry.
A distinctive feature of three-dimensional gauge
theories is the presence of an additional global 0-form symmetry [79, 80], known as the
topological or magnetic symmetry8. For a U(1) gauge theory with field strength F = dA,
the Bianchi identity dF = 0 implies conservation of the current [82]

Since this conservation law holds identically
independent of the equations of motion, the symmetry is topological.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
Monopole Operators
It has become increasingly clear in recent years that the set of local operators in a quantum
field theory is not exhausted by operators constructed as polynomials of the microscopic
fields appearing in the Lagrangian [90, 91]. In addition to these conventional order oper-
ators, one must also include so-called disorder (or defect) operators, which are defined by
prescribing suitable singular boundary conditions in the path integral.
At first sight, local disorder operators appear to be fundamentally different from standard
local operators, since their definition involves modifying the functional integration rather
than inserting a local composite operator. However, this distinction is largely an artifact
of the particular choice of field variables used to describe the quantum field theory. In the
full quantum theory, all local operators are on the same footing. This is reflected in the
existence of numerous dualities that map order operators to disorder operators, and vice
versa10.
In three-dimensional gauge theories, the relevant local disorder operators are monopole
operators [96].
These operators are naturally defined in the Euclidean formulation of
the theory and can be viewed as arising from dimensional reduction of ’t Hooft loop
operators in four dimensions.
Operationally, inserting a monopole operator M into a
correlation function amounts to integrating over gauge field configurations that exhibit a
Dirac monopole singularity at the insertion point. More precisely, for a monopole operator
of magnetic charge m, one defines [97–100]

In the absence of Chern–Simons interactions, a bare monopole—that is, a chiral operator
Mm defined solely in terms of the vector multiplet—can be made gauge invariant by
averaging over the action of the Weyl group.
10These include sine-Gordon–massive Thirring duality [92, 93], T-duality and mirror symmetry [94, 95]
in two spacetime dimensions, and mirror symmetry and Aharony duality in three spacetime dimensions.

Chapter 1
More generally, the singular boundary conditions imposed on A± and σ in the definition
of a monopole operator break the gauge group G to a residual subgroup
H ⊂G ,
defined as the stabilizer of the magnetic flux m, namely the subgroup satisfying H·m = 0.
In the monopole background, the vector multiplets associated with H remain massless,
while those corresponding to the coset G/H become massive. Upon integrating out these
massive modes, quantum corrections are induced that modify the global and gauge charges
of the monopole operator.
A similar mechanism applies to matter fields. In the presence of a monopole background,
matter fields that are neutral under the unbroken U(1) ⊂G×GF may acquire expectation
values without breaking supersymmetry. These fields can therefore be used to dress the
bare monopole operator. Concretely, one constructs dressed monopole operators by mul-
tiplying the bare, generally gauge-variant monopole by gauge-invariant polynomials of the
residual massless matter fields, yielding operators invariant under the unbroken subgroup
H.
Finally, full G-invariance is restored by averaging over the coset of Weyl groups WG/WH.
The presentation of monopole operators as disorder operators reflects a more modern
viewpoint on N = 2 gauge theories; historically, this was not the perspective adopted
in the earliest studies of mirror symmetry [29]. In the original interpretation, monopole
operators were instead motivated by a semiclassical analysis of the low-energy degrees of
freedom of the theory.
To illustrate this viewpoint, consider a U(1) gauge theory in three dimensions. As dis-
cussed earlier, the gauge field can be dualized to a compact scalar γ, known as the dual
photon. Together with the real scalar σ in the N = 2 vector multiplet, one may form the
complex field
Φ = σ + iγ .
In a semiclassical regime where the expectation value of Φ is large and the theory is
weakly coupled, monopole operators admit a simple effective description. In this limit,
a monopole operator carrying magnetic charge m can be identified with the exponential
operator11
Mm ∼e mΦ ,
which creates a configuration with quantized magnetic flux and carries charge under the
topological U(1)T symmetry [28].
From this perspective, monopole operators appear as ordinary local operators constructed
from the effective fields of the theory, rather than as disorder operators defined by singular
boundary conditions. The modern definition of monopole operators as disorder operators
can be understood as a fully non-perturbative formulation of this semiclassical picture,
valid throughout the quantum theory and in particular at strong coupling.

Statistical Transmutation via Flux Attachment:
In the presence of a monopole
background, the spin of fundamental fields in the ground state undergoes a shift — a phe-
nomenon known as statistical transmutation [110] (see also [111–116]). This shift depends
on the GNO charge (or flux) of the monopole, the intrinsic spin of the particle, and its
gauge charge.
In what follows, we summarize the effects of statistical transmutation for a particle of
spin s, and examine how this behavior is modified in the presence of multiple U(1) gauge
factors, particularly in the context of the quiver gauge theories discussed in the main text.

Moduli Space of Vacua
An important and characteristic feature of supersymmetric gauge theories, in three dimen-
sions as well as in higher dimensions, is the existence of continuous families of degenerate
vacuum solutions.
These arise when the equations of motion admit configurations in
which the vacuum expectation values (VEVs) of scalar fields are not uniquely fixed, but
instead vary continuously without costing energy. The space parametrized by these VEVs
is known as the moduli space of vacua [118].

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
Moduli spaces play a central role in both the physical and mathematical study of supersym-
metric field theories. Physically, they govern the low-energy dynamics, encode patterns
of spontaneous symmetry breaking, and determine the spectrum of massless degrees of
freedom around a given vacuum. Mathematically, they are often realized as geometric
spaces with rich structure—such as Kähler or hyperkähler manifolds—whose properties
are tightly constrained by supersymmetry. The existence and structure of moduli spaces
therefore provide one of the most powerful tools for analyzing supersymmetric gauge the-
ories.
In a supersymmetric gauge theory, a moduli space of vacua arises whenever the scalar
potential admits continuous families of zero-energy configurations.
In supersymmetric
theories, the scalar potential takes the universal form [119]
V =

solutions—modulo gauge transformations—defines the moduli space of supersymmetric
vacua.
In three-dimensional N = 4 supersymmetric gauge theories, the space of supersymmetric
vacua generically decomposes into distinct branches [31, 120]:
• Higgs Branch: The Higgs branch is defined as the space of vacua in which the
scalar fields in the matter (hypermultiplet) sector acquire non-vanishing expectation
values, while the scalar components of the vector multiplet vanish. Supersymmetry
then requires the vanishing of both F-terms and D-terms, and the moduli space is
parametrized by gauge-invariant composite operators, such as mesons and baryons,
subject to classical constraints. At the classical level, the Higgs branch is described
exactly by the solutions to these constraints. In N = 4 theories, the Higgs branch
is fully protected against quantum corrections by the enhanced R-symmetry and
remains exact at the quantum level [121]. The Higgs branch of N = 4 theories is a
hyperkähler manifold, obtained as a hyperkähler quotient by the gauge group [122,
123].
• Coulomb Branch: The Coulomb branch is defined as the space of vacua in which
the scalar fields in the matter multiplets have vanishing expectation values, while the

Chapter 1
scalar components of the vector multiplet acquire non-zero expectation values. As a
result, the gauge symmetry is generically broken by adjoint Higgsing to a subgroup
containing Abelian factors, with special loci where non-Abelian symmetry may be
restored.
As mentioned before, in three dimensions, each unbroken U(1) factor gives rise to
a dual photon γ, a compact scalar defined by ⋆F = dγ. The natural holomorphic
coordinates on the Coulomb branch are provided by monopole operators, which can
be viewed semiclassically as exponentials of the complex combinations σ +iγ. While
the Coulomb branch exists as a moduli space at the quantum level, its metric and
operator dimensions generally receive quantum corrections [124–126]. The Coulomb
branch of N = 4 theories is also a hyperkähler manifold due to the existence of the
dual photon.
As the N = 4 action contains no mixed terms involving the scalars of the matter and
vector multiplets, the moduli space of N = 4 theories is a product M = MC × MH.
A First Look at Infrared Dualities
As mentioned previously, local order and disorder operators in three-dimensional gauge
theories are on an equal footing in the quantum theory, and any apparent distinction
between them is largely an artifact of the choice of microscopic field variables used to
define the Lagrangian. This observation has profound consequences for the infrared (IR)
dynamics of such theories and is reflected in the existence of numerous nontrivial infrared
dualities.
Infrared dualities relate pairs of ultraviolet (UV) theories with seemingly different gauge
groups, matter content, and interactions, which nevertheless flow to the same interacting
fixed point at long distances. At the IR fixed point, the two descriptions become physically
indistinguishable: they share the same global symmetries, spectrum of local operators, cor-
relation functions, and moduli spaces of vacua. Crucially, duality maps gauge-invariant
operators in one theory to gauge-invariant operators in the dual description, often exchang-
ing elementary fields with composite operators and, in three dimensions, order operators
with disorder (monopole) operators.
Among the most well-studied examples of three-dimensional infrared dualities are the
so-called Aharony-like dualities [18, 20, 21, 127]. These dualities relate N = 2 supersym-
metric gauge theories to dual theories with different gauge groups and matter content,
supplemented by additional gauge-singlet fields and superpotential couplings.
A defining feature of Aharony-like dualities is the exact matching of global symmetries
across the dual pair, including both flavor symmetries and topological symmetries. Oper-
ators charged under these symmetries are mapped consistently between the two descrip-
tions. In particular, monopole operators parametrizing the Coulomb branch of one theory
are mapped to elementary or composite gauge-singlet fields in the dual theory, while Higgs-
branch operators are mapped to Higgs-branch operators. The duality therefore provides
distinct ultraviolet realizations of the same infrared fixed point, without exchanging the
roles of the Higgs and Coulomb branches.

Supersymmetric Localization and Observables
Supersymmetric localization [129–134] has emerged as one of the most powerful tools for
the exact study of observables in supersymmetric quantum field theories. Its central insight
is that, in the presence of sufficient supersymmetry, certain observables can be computed
exactly, receiving contributions only from a restricted set of field configurations.
16A useful operation that frequently appears in three-dimensional supersymmetric dualities is the flip
of a chiral operator. Given a gauge-invariant chiral operator O, one introduces a new gauge-singlet chiral
multiplet S := F[O] and adds a superpotential coupling
Wflip = S O.
The resulting F-term condition sets O = 0 in the chiral ring, thereby removing it as an independent
operator and replacing it with the elementary field S, which carries the same global quantum numbers.

Chapter 1
In general, the full dynamical content of a quantum field theory is encoded in its Feyn-
man path integral, an infinite-dimensional integral over the space of field configurations.
While this object is conceptually fundamental, it is typically intractable to evaluate ex-
actly, except in perturbative regimes. Supersymmetric localization provides a remarkable
mechanism by which this infinite-dimensional integral can be reduced to a much simpler
expression, often amenable to exact evaluation.
The key ingredient underlying localization is supersymmetry. For supersymmetric theo-
ries—either topologically twisted or defined on curved manifolds admitting rigid super-
symmetry—one can deform the action by a supersymmetry-exact term without changing
the value of the partition function or of suitably protected observables. By tuning the coef-
ficient of this deformation, the path integral localizes onto the locus of field configurations
that are fixed under the chosen supercharge, known as the localization locus. Fluctuations
around this locus are captured exactly by one-loop determinants.
From a mathematical perspective, supersymmetric localization can be viewed as a field-
theoretic generalization of equivariant localization for finite-dimensional integrals, where
integrals invariant under a symmetry group receive contributions only from the fixed
points of the group action. In the quantum field theory setting, this principle leads to a
dramatic reduction in complexity: a path integral over D-dimensional fields may reduce to
an effective path integral over d-dimensional fields, with d < D. In particularly favorable
cases—such as when the localization locus consists of constant field configurations—this
further collapses to a finite-dimensional integral, or even to a discrete sum over fixed
points.
Physically, localization may be understood as a situation in which the semiclassical (saddle-
point or one-loop) approximation becomes exact. Importantly, this exactness does not
arise from taking the original coupling ¯h to zero, but instead from introducing an auxiliary
deformation parameter whose vanishing limit enforces localization without altering the
value of the path integral.
Supersymmetric localization therefore yields non-perturbative, exact expressions for par-
tition functions and protected observables. These results provide powerful probes of the
infrared dynamics of supersymmetric gauge theories, enable precision tests of infrared du-
alities, and often admit elegant mathematical interpretations. In the following, we will not
attempt a systematic review of localization techniques; rather, we will directly present the
specific partition functions and observables that are relevant for the applications discussed
in this thesis.

When computed using the superconformal R-symmetry, this partition function coincides

As a result, the index is protected from quantum corrections and is an RG-invariant
observable, making it a powerful tool for probing infrared dynamics and testing proposed
infrared dualities.

Brane Dynamics and Gauge Theories
Brane configurations in string theory provide a powerful geometric framework for engi-
neering and analyzing supersymmetric quantum field theories. By realizing gauge degrees
of freedom as the low-energy dynamics of D-branes and matter fields as arising from open
strings stretched between them, one obtains a concrete and often intuitive description
of strongly coupled phenomena that are otherwise difficult to access using purely field-
theoretic methods. In this approach, properties such as moduli spaces of vacua, global
symmetries, and infrared dualities admit a direct geometric interpretation in terms of the
relative positions and orientations of branes.
In this section, we review the basic brane constructions relevant for three-dimensional
supersymmetric gauge theories (without justifying their string origins in much detail),
focusing primarily on Type IIB Hanany–Witten setups. These configurations naturally
realize N = 4 and N = 2 gauge theories in three dimensions and provide a unified frame-
work for understanding mirror symmetry, real mass and Fayet–Iliopoulos deformations,
and a variety of infrared dualities. The results reviewed here will serve as the geometric
underpinning for the mirror symmetry and duality constructions discussed in the following
chapters. This section collects results from [153–160].
17An important criterion for 3d N = 4 SCFTs is whether they are good, bad or ugly [150], which indicates
whether the UV and IR R-charges coincide. For N = 4 gauge theories, a single gauge group G with Nf
hypermultiplets is considered good if
for U(N): Nf ≥2N,
for SO(N): Nf ≥N −1,
for USp(2N): Nf ≥2N + 1.
Good theories are those in which all monopole operators obey the unitarity bound, and the theory flows
to an interacting SCFT in the infrared. Ugly theories contain some monopole operators that satisfy the
unitarity bound, and the theory flows to an interacting SCFT with some free multiplets at the IR FP.
Monopole operators in bad theories violate the unitarity bound, and the infrared physics of these theories
is unclear.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
The Zoology of Dp-Branes
In addition to the fundamental string, string theory contains extended p-dimensional ob-
jects known as p-branes. At weak string coupling gs, these objects can be broadly clas-
sified according to how their tension scales with gs. Solitonic, or Neveu–Schwarz (NS),
branes have tension scaling as T ∼g−2
s , while Dirichlet (D) branes have tension scaling
as T ∼g−1
s . This distinction is central to the non-perturbative structure of string theory
and underlies many duality relations.
In the low-energy supergravity description, p-branes appear as extended objects electrically
charged under (p + 1)-form gauge fields. We follow the notation of [154]. The (9 + 1)-
dimensional spacetime of string theory is labeled by coordinates (x0, x1, . . . , x9), with
associated Dirac matrices ΓM (M = 0, . . . , 9).
Type IIA string theory possesses (1, 1) spacetime supersymmetry, with left- and right-
moving supercharges satisfying
Γ0 · · · Γ9 QL = +QL,
Γ0 · · · Γ9 QR = −QR.
Type IIB string theory, by contrast, has (2, 0) spacetime supersymmetry, with both su-
percharges obeying
Γ0 · · · Γ9 QL = +QL,
Γ0 · · · Γ9 QR = +QR.
As a consequence, Type IIA string theory is non-chiral, whereas Type IIB string theory
is chiral.
The spectrum of string theory consists of a finite number of light degrees of freedom
together with an infinite tower of massive excitations, whose masses are of order the string
scale or higher. To make contact with low-energy physics, it is therefore convenient to
focus on the dynamics of the light modes. This is achieved by integrating out the massive
string excitations, yielding an effective field theory governing the low-energy degrees of
freedom.
Applying this procedure to Type II string theory leads to two distinct ten-dimensional
low-energy effective theories: Type IIA and Type IIB supergravity. In both cases, the
field content decomposes into a Neveu–Schwarz–Neveu–Schwarz (NS–NS) sector, common
to the two theories, containing the spacetime metric gMN, the Kalb–Ramond two-form
BMN, and the dilaton Φ. The Ramond–Ramond (R–R) sector differs between the two
theories: Type IIA supergravity contains odd-degree form potentials, while Type IIB
supergravity contains even-degree form potentials. Correspondingly, the brane content of
the two theories is
• Type IIA: Dp-branes with even p, namely D0, D2, D4, D6, and D8 branes.
• Type IIB: Dp-branes with odd p, namely D(−1), D1, D3, D5, and D7 branes. The
D(−1) brane is special, as it corresponds to a D-instanton [161].
From a microscopic perspective, D-branes are defined as hypersurfaces on which funda-
mental strings can end [153]. A Dp-brane extended along the (x1, . . . , xp) directions and
localized at a point in (xp+1, . . . , x9) is characterized by Neumann boundary conditions
on the open-string endpoints along (x1, . . . , xp) and Dirichlet boundary conditions along
(xp+1, . . . , x9).

2-BPS objects, meaning that they preserve half of the thirty-

ϵL = Γ0Γ1 · · · Γp ϵR.

The observation that a stack of N coincident Dp-branes gives rise to a U(N) super–Yang–Mills
theory with sixteen supercharges in (p+1) dimensions lies at the heart of the geometric en-
gineering of gauge theories. In this framework, gauge symmetries, matter content, and cou-
pling constants acquire a direct geometric interpretation in terms of brane configurations
and background fields. As a result, intrinsically non-perturbative phenomena in quan-
tum field theory—such as infrared dualities, symmetry enhancement, and strong-coupling
dynamics—can often be analyzed using classical or semiclassical string-theoretic tools.
Geometric engineering thus provides a powerful and unifying perspective on the structure
of supersymmetric gauge theories. In what follows, we will study three-dimensional gauge
theories from this perspective.
Geometric Engineering of Three-Dimensional Results
Picking up from the discussion of D-branes and their worldvolume dynamics, we now turn
to the geometric engineering of three-dimensional gauge theories in string theory. The
central idea of geometric engineering is that the low-energy dynamics of a quantum field
theory can be realized as the effective description on the worldvolume of branes embedded
in a suitable string-theoretic background. By appropriately choosing the type of branes,
their dimensionality, and their relative orientations, one can engineer supersymmetric
gauge theories with a prescribed amount of supersymmetry, matter content, and global
symmetries.
Three-dimensional gauge theories are particularly well suited to this approach. Many of
their characteristic features—such as monopole operators, topological symmetries, Chern–
Simons interactions, and intricate webs of infrared dualities—admit a natural and often
geometric interpretation in terms of brane configurations.
N = 4 Theories
To study three-dimensional gauge dynamics, we consider, following [19], configurations of
N D3-branes suspended between NS5-branes and intersecting F D5-branes, arranged as
in Table 1.5. This system realizes a large class of three-dimensional supersymmetric gauge
theories as the low-energy effective theories living on the D3-branes.
We consider two infinite NS5-branes separated by a distance L6 in the x6 direction, located
at the same point in (x7, x8, x9). N D3-branes are suspended between them in the x6
direction. This configuration is shown schematically in Figure 1.46.

where SO(1, 2) acts on the common directions (x0, x1, x2), one SO(3) factor acts on the

The SO(1, 2)
factor is interpreted as the Lorentz symmetry of the (2 + 1)-dimensional worldvolume
theory on the D3-branes, while the two SO(3) factors are global symmetries. In the low-
energy theory, these are identified with the SU(2)C × SU(2)H R-symmetry of N = 4
supersymmetry.
To analyze the low-energy physics of this configuration, it is useful to distinguish between
three classes of excitations:
• bulk modes propagating in the ten-dimensional spacetime;
• modes localized on the NS5-branes;
• modes localized on the D3-branes.
For an observer restricted to the (2+1)-dimensional directions (x0, x1, x2), the bulk modes
and the excitations living on the NS5-branes are effectively frozen at low energies in the
weak string coupling limit. By contrast, the degrees of freedom living on the N D3-branes
remain dynamical, since the D3-branes have finite extent along the x6 direction.
The worldvolume theory on the D3-branes is therefore naturally viewed as a four-dimensional
theory on R1,2×I, where I is an interval of finite length L6 along the x6 direction, bounded
by NS5-branes. At energy scales much smaller than 1/L6, the theory reduces effectively
to a (2 + 1)-dimensional quantum field theory.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
The light degrees of freedom on a stack of N coincident D3-branes consist of a four-
dimensional U(N) gauge field Aµ, with µ = 0, 1, 2, 6, together with six real scalar fields
transforming in the adjoint representation of U(N). These scalars correspond to transverse
fluctuations of the D3-branes in the directions
(X3, X4, X5, X7, X8, X9).
The presence of the NS5-branes imposes boundary conditions on the fields at the ends of
the interval I. In particular, the fluctuations of the D3-branes in the (x7, x8, x9) directions
are frozen at the NS5-brane boundaries, while the scalars (X3, X4, X5) remain dynamical.
As a result, Kaluza–Klein modes with masses of order 1/L6 decouple in the low-energy
limit, and the remaining massless degrees of freedom organize themselves into a three-
dimensional N = 4 vector multiplet, consisting of the U(N) gauge field Aµ (µ = 0, 1, 2)
and the three adjoint scalars (X3, X4, X5), with gauge coupling g2
Y M ∼gs
L6 .
Additional matter fields arise from open strings stretching between the D3- and D5-branes.
Each D5-brane intersecting the D3-branes contributes a three-dimensional N = 4 hyper-
multiplet transforming in the fundamental representation of U(N). Thus, a configuration
with F D5-branes realizes a U(N) gauge theory with F fundamental hypermultiplets.
Geometric parameters of the brane configuration are naturally mapped to field theory
couplings.
Relative positions of D5-branes in the (x3, x4, x5) directions correspond to
real mass parameters for the hypermultiplets. Similarly, separations of NS5-branes in the
(x7, x8, x9) directions give rise to Fayet–Iliopoulos parameters.
The positions of the N D3-branes stretched between the NS5-branes in the transverse
directions (x3, x4, x5), together with the N dual photons arising from the unbroken U(1)N
gauge symmetry, parametrize the 4N-dimensional Coulomb branch of the three-dimensional
N = 4 gauge theory. These moduli correspond to vacuum expectation values of the scalars
in the vector multiplet and their dual photons.
From the perspective of the fivebrane worldvolume theory, however, this moduli space
admits a different and highly instructive interpretation. The theory living on the pair of
NS5-branes is a six-dimensional N = (1, 1) supersymmetric gauge theory with gauge group
U(2), which is spontaneously broken to U(1)×U(1) by the separation of the fivebranes. In
this description, D3-branes stretched between the NS5-branes are identified with magnetic
monopole strings of the broken SU(2) gauge theory [167].
Consequently, the 4N-dimensional Coulomb branch of the three-dimensional theory is nat-
urally identified, from the fivebrane viewpoint, with the moduli space of N BPS monopoles.

Aharony Duality from Geometric Engineering.
Aharony duality admits a natural
and geometrically transparent realization in the Hanany–Witten brane construction of
three-dimensional N = 2 supersymmetric gauge theories. We demonstrate this here by
recovering Aharony duality from the N = 2 setup of Table 1.6.
A key ingredient in the brane realization of the duality is the Hanany–Witten effect [19].
When the NS5′-brane is adiabatically moved past a D5-brane along the x6 direction, a D3-
brane is created stretching between the NS5′-brane and the D5-brane in order to preserve
charge conservation and supersymmetry. As the NS5′-brane crosses all Nf D5-branes, a
total of Nf D3-branes are created. Taking into account the original Nc color D3-branes,
the resulting configuration contains Nf −Nc D3-branes suspended between the NS5 and
NS5′ branes, realizing a dual U(Nf −Nc) gauge theory at low energies.
In addition to the color branes, the brane crossing produces semi-infinite D3-branes
stretching between the NS5′-brane and the D5-branes. From the three-dimensional per-
spective, these degrees of freedom are non-dynamical gauge singlets. Their presence is
essential: they are naturally identified with the chiral multiplets that are dual to the
electric monopole operators. Geometrically, monopole operators correspond to D1-strings
stretched between the NS5 and NS5′ branes, and the couplings between these D1-strings
and the semi-infinite D3-branes induce superpotential interactions that flip the monopoles
of the dual theory.
An important consistency condition in this construction is the s-rule (also see [168]),
which states that at most a single supersymmetric D3-brane can stretch between an NS5-
brane and a D5-brane while preserving supersymmetry. In the present context, the s-
rule ensures that the number of color D3-branes suspended between the NS5 and NS5′

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
branes is bounded by Nf, and in particular that a supersymmetric vacuum exists only
when Nf ≥Nc.
This constraint has a direct field-theoretic interpretation: it mirrors
the condition for the existence of a stable supersymmetric vacuum in three-dimensional
N = 2 SQCD and guarantees that the brane configuration correctly encodes the infrared
dynamics of the theory.
Thus, we see that embedding QFTs in string theory sheds light on a lot of properties
which are not a priori visible. Furthermore, the geometric properties of string theory are
such that it helps relate QFTs in ways far from obvious.
The Hanany–Witten construction not only provides a geometric realization of Aharony
duality, but also furnishes a unifying framework in which a wide class of three-dimensional
dualities arise from simple operations on brane configurations. Among these, mirror sym-
metry occupies a distinguished position, admitting a particularly transparent geometric
interpretation in Type IIB string theory. Mirror symmetry is the central theme of this
thesis, and we will review its basic structure and physical implications in greater detail in
the following section.

Mirror Symmetry in Three Dimensions
Unlike ordinary perturbative symmetries, which are visible order by order in a weak-
coupling expansion, a nonperturbative quantum symmetry [172] is not manifest at the per-
turbative level. In general, establishing such a symmetry would require complete control
over the full quantum theory, including its strongly coupled regime. What makes duality
symmetries particularly powerful is that, in supersymmetric theories, they can often be
inferred using only weak-coupling and semiclassical data, without detailed knowledge of
the full nonperturbative dynamics.
Supersymmetry imposes strong constraints on the structure of quantum corrections. In
theories with sufficient supersymmetry, many semiclassical quantities receive no quantum
corrections—neither perturbative nor nonperturbative—and are therefore exact.
Such
protected quantities can be computed reliably in the weak-coupling regime and then ana-
lytically continued to strong coupling. If two theories are related by a duality, all nonrenor-
malized observables must agree. The requirement that these quantities match provides
highly nontrivial consistency checks, which are often compelling enough to establish the
existence of a duality even in the absence of a rigorous proof [173].

Chapter 1
Type IIB string theory provides a canonical example of this phenomenon. In addition to
discrete Z2 perturbative symmetries, it enjoys a nonperturbative SL(2; Z) duality sym-
metry, which is visible at the level of the equations of motion governing the axio–dilaton
field. The modular group SL(2; Z) is generated by the elements
S =

Provided that strong-coupling effects do not break supersymmetry, consistency implies
that Type IIB string theory can only be dual to itself under this symmetry.
There are two classes of semiclassical quantities that are expected to be exact and must
therefore exhibit the SL(2; Z) duality group:
1. the massless spectrum and the corresponding equations of motion;
2. the spectrum of BPS-saturated states.
Consequently, one expects the nonperturbative SL(2; Z) invariance of Type IIB string
theory to manifest itself as a duality symmetry in Type IIB Hanany–Witten brane con-
figurations.
We focus on the S ∈SL(2; Z) element, which implements a strong–weak duality by acting
on the Type IIB coupling as gs 7→1/gs. In brane language, the action of S on the objects
appearing in an N = 4 Hanany–Witten setup is
D3 −→D3,
NS5 −→D5,
D5 −→−NS5.
Here the minus sign indicates a reversal of orientation, which is irrelevant for most field-
theoretic considerations but becomes important when keeping track of Chern–Simons cou-
plings and linking numbers.
Since the D3-branes are self-dual under S, while NS5- and D5-branes are exchanged,
the action of S effectively interchanges the directions (x3, x4, x5) and (x7, x8, x9) in the
standard Hanany–Witten configuration (Table 1.5). At the level of the three-dimensional
worldvolume theory, this induces an automorphism of the N = 4 supersymmetry algebra
that exchanges the two SU(2) factors of the R-symmetry,
SU(2)C ←→SU(2)H.
More precisely, in the Hanany–Witten construction, NS5-branes engineer vector multiplet
degrees of freedom on the D3-branes, while D5-branes engineer hypermultiplet matter.
The S transformation therefore exchanges the sectors of the theory associated with vector
multiplets and hypermultiplets. At the level of the moduli space of vacua, this corresponds
to an exact exchange of the Coulomb and Higgs branches (both of which are hyperkähler
manifolds [174, 175]) at the infrared fixed point.
The central statement of N = 4 mirror symmetry [19, 23, 176–178] is thus that it
maps perturbative Higgs-branch operators, such as mesons and baryons, to intrinsically
nonperturbative Coulomb-branch operators, namely monopole operators. From this per-
spective, mirror symmetry can be understood as an infrared duality that exchanges flavor
symmetries with topological symmetries of the three-dimensional theory.
It is important to stress that this mechanism is qualitatively different from Aharony-like
dualities in three dimensions. While Aharony duality relates distinct ultraviolet gauge

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
theories that flow to the same infrared fixed point, it does not exchange topological and
flavor symmetries.
In contrast, N = 4 mirror symmetry precisely interchanges these
symmetries, mapping flavor currents on one side to topological currents on the other.
Mirror symmetry is therefore a distinct type of infrared duality, characterized by an exact
exchange of Higgs and Coulomb branch data rather than a rearrangement of matter content
within a fixed global symmetry structure.

The global symmetry of the U(N) SQCD with F flavors, depicted

SU(F) × U(1)η × U(1)τ,
