The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
This enhancement has been verified using a variety of complementary methods: matching
the infrared metrics on Higgs and Coulomb branches21 [177], comparing S3
b partition func-
tions [179], and studying correlation functions and operator products of Coulomb branch
operators in the one-dimensional topological sector [180].
Mapping of gauge-invariant operators.
Mirror symmetry also provides a precise
correspondence between gauge-invariant operators of the two theories [99, 125]. In partic-
ular,
• The fundamental monopole operators M± of the SQCD theory are mapped to long
mesonic operators on the mirror quiver side.
• The electric mesons, transforming in the traceless adjoint representation of SU(F),
are mapped to a collection of monopole operators in the mirror theory. These as-
semble into an adjoint-valued matrix of SU(F) constructed from the traces of the
adjoint fields ΦJ together with monopole operators carrying GNO flux under con-
secutive gauge nodes: monopoles with flux ±1 under a single node, monopoles with
flux ±1 under two adjacent nodes, and so on, up to monopoles carrying flux ±1
under all F −1 gauge nodes.
Mirror symmetry has a natural interpretation for theories with N = 4 supersymmetry,
and is seen as a consequence of S-duality inherited from IIB string theory. As such, it is
an infrared duality that relates theories with swapped flavor and topological symmetries.
What makes theories with N < 4 so intractable?
The subject of this thesis is
the study of mirror symmetry in three-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories with
N < 4 supersymmetry. This regime is substantially more subtle than the well-understood
N = 4 case. As we had mentioned before, N = 4 mirror symmetry may be intuitively
seen as an automorphism on the supersymmetry algebra that exchanges the two SU(2)
factors of the R-symmetry. When the amount of supersymmetry is reduced, this intuition
fails, as the R-symmetry does not have identical factors (e.g.: the R-symmetry of N = 3
theories is SO(3) ∼= SU(2), while that of N = 2 theories is SO(2) ∼= U(1)) that can be
exchanged.
More precisely, when the amount of supersymmetry is reduced, the Higgs and Coulomb
branches may not factorize into distinct product manifolds, or may be partially or com-
pletely lifted, and the canonical Type IIB brane realizations that underlie mirror symmetry
are often no longer available22.
As a consequence, mirror duals of N < 4 theories are not known in general and cannot
be systematically derived from brane constructions. Their identification therefore requires
intrinsically field-theoretic tools. The aim of this thesis is to develop such methods and to
propose and test mirror-like dualities in broad classes of three-dimensional theories with
reduced supersymmetry.
Throughout this work, we are guided by the defining feature of mirror symmetry: it is a
duality that exchanges topological and flavor symmetries, mapping order and
21This result was stated in a more mathematically precise language by [22], and we repeat it here: the
Higgs branch of Kronheimer [122] gauge theories is an almost-locally Euclidean (ALE) space, while the
Coulomb branch gives the moduli space of a small ADE instanton. In this sense, mirror symmetry shows
ALE gravitational instantons and the moduli space of ADE instantons are naturally dual to each other.
22It was shown in [26] that applying S−duality on N = 2 brane setups (like the one shown in Figure
1.47) is possible, but the resulting dual description is inherently non-Lagrangian since it is built with
superconformal matter.

Chapter 1
disorder operators to each other. Thus, even when geometric engineering techniques
no longer exist, constraints from arguments based on symmetries are strong enough for us
to generalize this notion to theories with minimal supersymmetry.

Part II: N = 2 Mirror Symmetry
in Unitary Gauge Theories

Chapter 2
Mirror Duals of N = 2 Unitary
Chern-Simons-Matter Theories
In this chapter, we develop a systematic framework to derive a new class of three-dimensional
N = 2 dualities, which we refer to as chiral-planar mirror dualities1 [2, 3]. These dualities
relate non-abelian chiral gauge theories with Chern-Simons interactions to purely abelian,
planar quiver gauge theories, and arise as infrared limits of real mass deformations of
known N = 4 mirror pairs [19, 22].
Our starting point is the observation that many N = 2 chiral theories of interest can be
obtained from N = 4 gauge theories by turning on a real mass deformation for the axial
symmetry
U(1)τ = U(1)H−C,
which breaks supersymmetry from N = 4 to N = 2 [25, 29].
A prototypical example is provided by three-dimensional N = 4 U(N) SQCD with F
hypermultiplets. By combining the axial real mass deformation with appropriate shifts
along the Coulomb branch, this theory flows to a chiral N = 2 U(N) SQCD with F
fundamental chiral multiplets and nontrivial Chern–Simons levels. Since the original N =
4 theory admits a mirror description in terms of a linear quiver [19], one may attempt
to perform the same deformation on the dual side. However, the mirror theory contains
many gauge nodes and hence many Coulomb branch directions, leading to a large set of
candidate vacua. Identifying the vacuum that correctly reproduces the dual of the chiral
SQCD is therefore highly nontrivial.
A powerful tool to address this problem is provided by the exact partition function on
the squashed three-sphere S3
b [139, 143]. As shown in [21, 171], real mass deformations
correspond to controlled large-mass limits of the S3
b partition function. In such limits,
the partition function factorizes into a finite contribution—associated with the interacting
infrared theory—and a highly oscillatory phase. Crucially, this phase depends on how the
real mass limit is combined with shifts of the gauge fugacities, and therefore encodes the
choice of vacuum on the Coulomb branch. As a consequence, matching the oscillatory
phases on the two sides of a known N = 4 mirror identity provides a sharp criterion to
identify the pair of infrared theories that remain dual after the deformation.
In this chapter, we apply this strategy in detail to the case of U(N) SQCD. Starting
from the equality of the S3
b partition functions of the N = 4 mirror pair, we perform a
1This was historically called the Dorito Duality by the author. Never forget what was taken from us.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
systematic real mass deformation and show how to isolate the vacuum that reproduces
the chiral N = 2 SQCD on the electric side. Remarkably, we find that the corresponding
magnetic theory is a planar2 abelian quiver, obtained by fully Higgsing each U(k) gauge
node of the N = 4 mirror theory down to its Cartan subgroup.
The resulting dual
theory exhibits nontrivial mixed Chern–Simons couplings and a rich pattern of topological
symmetry enhancement, inherited from the parent N = 4 mirror symmetry.
Beyond establishing this specific duality, the analysis presented here highlights a general
structural feature: real mass deformations of N = 4 mirror pairs naturally lead to chiral–
planar mirror dualities, in which mesonic symmetries are mapped to topological symme-
tries and abelian gauge dynamics emerge in a planar arrangement. While the deformation
procedure itself is technically involved, it provides valuable guidance for identifying the
correct infrared dual and motivates the search for a more systematic construction.
This motivation leads naturally to the algorithmic approach developed in the subsequent
half of the chapter, where we extend the mirror dualization algorithm [187, 188] to the
chiral–planar setting. The detailed partition-function analysis presented here thus serves
a dual purpose: it establishes the validity of the chiral–planar mirror duality in a concrete
example, and it provides the conceptual and technical foundation for the algorithmic
framework that follows.
General Yoga of Planar Abelianization
We pick up where we left off in the previous chapter while reviewing 3d N = 4 mirror
symmetry in U(N) gauge theories with F hypermultiplets, shown in the top row of Figure
We remind the reader we are working in the N = 2∗set-up [25] to effectively study
supersymmetry breaking real mass deformations. Thus, we set U(1)R = U(1)C+H as the
R-symmetry group, where U(1)C ∈SU(2)C and U(1)H ∈SU(2)H of the non-abelian
N = 4 R-symmetry SU(2)H × SU(2)C. The commutant of U(1)R, which is U(1)τ =
U(1)C−H, is a global symmetry from the point of view of the N = 2 theory. Thus, the
global symmetries of both theories is SU(F) × U(1)top × U(1)τ.
We now consider a supersymmetry breaking mass deformation. This choice is not ar-
bitrary, and we do so by turning on a non-zero vacuum expectation value (VEV) for
the real scalar component of the background vector multiplet associated with the U(1)τ
symmetry. The VEV induces a mass term for every chiral superfield proportional to its
charge under the symmetry. Recalling that U(1)τ is a flavor subgroup of the N = 4 R-
symmetry; hence, the deformation performed has the effect of breaking the R-symmetry
from SU(2)C × SU(2)H →U(1)R, and supersymmetry is broken from N = 4 to N = 2.
In the most generic vacuum, the resulting IR theory would be a topological quantum field
theory. However, we follow the flow to distinguished points on the Coulomb branch where
the theory is an interacting one instead. While non-trivial, this can be done effectively
at the level of the S3
b partition function [139, 143] and the equations of motion [31, 189].
However, before doing this, we will attempt to qualitatively motivate this picture first.

Global Symmetries and Gauge Invariant Operators
We now comment on the features of the duality described above. As a preliminary check,
we compute the Superconformal Index [140, 191]. This guarantees that a number of
properties are correctly reproduced by the dual theory such as - the presence of conserved
currents associated to the correct global symmetry, and the properties of the operators
in the chiral ring. We do this for N = 2, F = 4, 5, 6 and N = 3 , F = 6, and report the
results in Table 2.1. Notice that in order to perform the matching, it is important to have
the mirror theory parameterized as in Figure 2.2. In particular the mixing between the
R-symmetry and the topological symmetries is crucial for the enhancement of the global
symmetry to be seen from the Superconformal Index.
Matching Global Symmetries
The global symmetry of the SQCD theory is, up to
discrete factors, SU(F) × U(1)η. On the mirror side, we can match the rank of the global
symmetry as follows - in the absence of the monopole superpotential there is a U(1)
topological symmetry for each U(1) gauge node. The monopole superpotential breaks the
topological symmetries associated to the nodes in a column of the quiver to a diagonal
U(1). Therefore there is a U(1)F−1 unbroken topological symmetry associated to the F −1
columns of the planar quiver. Furthermore, there is a single U(1)η flavor symmetry which
is preserved by the planar superpotential. In total, the rank of the global symmetry is F,
matching the SQCD side.
The mirror-like duality implies that the U(1)F symmetry of the quiver theory is enhanced
to SU(F) × U(1), as can be seen from the index calculations as well.
Mapping Chiral Ring Operators
The chiral ring of the planar theory is generated
by a single mesonic operator, constructed by contracting the F diagonal bifundamental
fields along a path starting from the U(1)η flavor node and ending at the opposite flavor
node. This operator carries trial R-charge 0 and U(1)η charge 1. Its uniqueness follows

Chapter 2
from the F-term relations imposed by the planar superpotential Wplanar.
From the superconformal index, we expect this mesonic operator to be mapped to a
monopole operator generating the chiral ring of the SQCD. The bare monopole operators
M±,0,...,0 are gauge variant, but they can be dressed to form gauge-invariant operators,
as reviewed in Chapter 1. We now argue that the relevant dressing involves the gaugino
fields.
Turning on GNO fluxes induces a monopole background in which the gauge group is
partially broken. For a fundamental monopole, the breaking pattern is
U(N) −→U(1) × U(N −1) .
Accordingly, all gauge-variant operators decompose into representations of the residual
gauge group. The branching rules for irreducible representations under U(N) ⊃U(N −
1) × U(1) are [192]
□N = (□N−1, 1) ⊕(1, 1 −N)

additional components transforming in the fundamental and anti-fundamental represen-

The View from the Equations of Motion
We now show that the flow from N = 4 to N = 2 mirror dualities described in Figure 2.1
is consistent with the equations of motion of the parent N = 4 theories. In particular, we
demonstrate that the vacua corresponding to the shifts in gauge fugacities satisfy both the
F-term and D-term equations [31, 127, 189]. Moreover, we show that fluctuations around
these vacua reproduce the equations of motion of the resulting N = 2 theories6. This
provides a non-trivial consistency check of our proposal.
Electric theory.
We begin by considering the N = 4 U(N) SQCD with F flavors.
When viewed as an N = 2 theory, its field content consists of F fundamental and anti-
fundamental chiral multiplets Q and ˜Q, together with an adjoint chiral multiplet Φ. We
also turn on a real FI parameter η.
6Following the analysis of [120], the solutions to the equations of motion come in four possible families:
• Higgs vacua, where only the scalar components of the chiral multiplets acquire a VEV, and span
a GLSM with a flag manifold as the target space.
• Topological vacua, where the real scalars in the vector multiplet acquire a VEV giving a mass to
the remaining matter content. The resulting theory in the infrared is a TQFT.
• Hybrid vacua, which are a mixture of the above two cases. Typically, these are products of TQFTs
and GLSMs on flag manifolds.
• Strongly coupled vacua arise only for specific values of the CS levels. These are encountered
near the origin of the classical Coulomb branch once the semiclassical approximation breaks down.

Chapter 2
This reproduces the vacuum described in (2.16). The resulting light degrees of freedom,
determined from (2.23), coincide with those obtained from the S3
b partition function anal-
ysis. Expanding the equations of motion around this vacuum also reproduces the correct
self and mixed Chern–Simons interactions of the planar theory in Figure 2.2. The planar
superpotential arises from the surviving F-term constraints, while the monopole superpo-
tential is not directly accessible from this analysis.
As a technical aside, we comment on the similarities and differences between the analysis
based on the S3
b partition function and that based on the equations of motion.
It can be verified that, under a real mass deformation parametrized by τ, the requirement
that the S3
b partition function factorizes into a divergent oscillating phase times a finite
integral is equivalent to solving the D-term equations up to O(τ 0). This correspondence
arises because large shifts of the gauge fugacities in the S3
b partition function precisely
capture motion far along the Coulomb branch of the moduli space.
By contrast, the S3
b partition function is insensitive to motion along the Higgs branch. As
a consequence, solving the remaining equations of motion provides an independent and
non-trivial check of the consistency of the real mass deformation considered above.
Finally, when multiple vacua are present, the analysis of the equations of motion alone
does not yield a systematic prescription for identifying how vacua are mapped across the
duality.
In this context, the divergent phase extracted from the S3
b partition function
plays a crucial role: it is sensitive to the spectrum of massive fields in a given vacuum
and therefore provides a powerful constraint on the correct matching of vacua under the
duality.
Shifting ℓvia Witten’s SL(2; Z) Action
In this chapter, we will primarily focus on dualities involving unitary gauge theories of
the form U(N)(k,k−N), namely theories in which the abelian Chern–Simons level ℓis fixed
to ℓ= −1. Nevertheless, it is often useful to relate these theories to their special-unitary
counterparts. In particular, an SU(N)k Chern–Simons theory can be obtained from a
U(N) theory by removing the diagonal U(1) factor. This is achieved by introducing a
background BF coupling between the topological symmetry U(1)Y and a new abelian
symmetry U(1)B, and subsequently gauging the topological symmetry.
This procedure is commonly referred to as the ungauging of the diagonal U(1) ⊂U(N).
Within the conventions adopted throughout this thesis, the ungauging operation is imple-

In what follows, we will make use of the ungauging operation (2.26) to derive the SU(N)

presented in Figure 2.2. On the electric side, this procedure yields N = 2 SU(N)−F

SQCD. On the mirror side, the topological symmetry of the unitary theory is realized as
a flavor symmetry acting on the bottom flavor node of the planar quiver. Consequently,
the ungauging operation described above translates into gauging this flavor node.
To implement this gauging consistently, it is essential to keep track of the background
Chern–Simons and BF terms that are not explicitly shown in Figure 2.2, but are instead
encoded in the S3
b partition function (2.17). Taking these contributions into account, one
arrives at the chiral–planar mirror duality depicted in Figure 2.3. This duality was first
presented in [2]7.
By gauging the baryonic symmetry in Figure 2.3, following the prescription in (2.27), we
recover the mirror duality for U(N)(k,k+N) SQCD. At this stage, one may further include
a Chern–Simons term at level ∆ℓfor the baryonic symmetry before gauging. This results
in a U(N) gauge theory with CS levels (k, k + N(1 + ∆ℓ)).
The combined effect of gauging and ungauging operations, together with the introduc-
tion of background Chern–Simons terms, realizes Witten’s SL(2; Z) action [87] on the
topological symmetry of the U(N) theory.
On the mirror side, this procedure introduces an additional U(1)∆ℓgauge node, which is
coupled to the rest of the planar quiver through a BF interaction. The resulting chiral–
planar mirror duality is shown in Figure 2.4.
For ∆ℓ= 0, ±1, the planar quiver in Figure 2.4 can be further simplified by explicitly
integrating out the additional gauge node.
When ∆ℓ= 0, the path integral over the extra node produces a delta function constraint.
This constraint fixes the gauge field of the node immediately to its right, which is therefore
demoted to a flavor node. As a result, one recovers the original planar mirror dual of
U(N)(−F
2 +N,−F

and is coupled to the theory as a flipper for a gauge-invariant monopole operator. This does not lead to a
cascade of sequential confinements, except in the special case N = 2.

Planar Abelian Duals of AN Quiver Gauge Theories
In this section, we focus on applying our findings to more general linear quiver gauge
theories. To that end, we pick the example of the FT[U(N)] quiver gauge theory [193]
as a starting point. This theory is closely related to the well-known T[SU(N)] theory
introduced in [150], differing only by the presence of an additional singlet chiral field
transforming in the adjoint representation of a global SU(N) symmetry and flipping the
Higgs-branch moment map. The FT[U(N)] theory plays a distinguished role in our anal-
ysis because it is self-mirror, a property that will be central to what follows. We will make
these ideas more precise below.
The FT[U(N)] theory is a strongly interacting SCFT with global symmetry SU(N) ⃗X ×
SU(N)⃗Y × U(1)τ 8. We denote this theory compactly:

The FT[U(N)] admits a second UV completion, as shown in Figure 2.6. In this alternative
description, the roles of the two SU(N) global symmetries are exchanged. In each com-

symmetry rotating the last bifundamental. The second SU(N) global symmetry instead
enhances from the N −1 topological symmetries due to all the nodes being balanced.
The two UV completions are related by mirror duality - in this case, these theories are
self-mirror since they describe the same IR fixed point.

A few comments regarding the structure of the superpotential and Chern–Simons interac-
tions of the planar quiver in Figure 2.9 are in order:
• For every closed triangular cycle in the planar quiver, there is a cubic superpotential
term.
These interactions descend directly from the cubic N = 4 superpotential
terms that are preserved by the real mass deformation. We collectively denote these
contributions by Wplanar.
The assignment of trial R-charges is as follows: diagonal chiral fields are assigned
R-charge r, while vertical chiral fields are assigned R-charge 2−r. This assignment is
consistent with the cubic planar superpotential. Moreover, the FI parameters depend
explicitly on the value of r, reflecting a mixing between the topological symmetries
and the R-symmetry.
• Due to the adjoint Higgsing of the gauge nodes, monopole operators appear in the
effective description. In particular, there is a linear monopole superpotential term
for each monopole carrying charges +1 and −1 under the upper and lower gauge
nodes connected by a vertical edge [190]. We denote these contributions collectively
by Wmonopole.
These monopole superpotential terms play a crucial role in the infrared dynamics:
they are essential for the enhancement of the topological U(1)N−1 symmetry to the
non-Abelian SU(N) global symmetry, as will be discussed later.
• The planar theory also includes both self and mixed Chern-Simons interactions.
Each gauge node carries a Chern-Simons level +1.
In addition, for each pair of
gauge nodes connected by a diagonal (respectively vertical) chiral multiplet, there is
a mixed Chern-Simons interaction with level −1 (respectively +1). All such Chern-
Simons terms arise from integrating out fermionic modes that become massive along
the real mass deformation.
• Finally, there are shifts in the FI parameters induced by the mixing between the
topological symmetries of the gauge nodes and the R-symmetry. The FI parameters
for the gauge nodes in the J-th column from the right take the form

Mapping Operators and Global Symmetries
We now comment on the duality map between global symmetries and the chiral rings of
the two theories.
Let us begin with the global symmetries. As stated at the beginning of this section, the
G[U(N)] SCFT has a U(1)N−1 × SU(N) global symmetry. In the chiral UV completion
(Figure 2.8), both factors of this symmetry are realized manifestly in the ultraviolet: the
SU(N) symmetry appears as a flavor symmetry acting on the matter fields, while the
U(1)N−1 symmetries correspond to the topological symmetries associated with the N −1
gauge nodes.
In contrast, in the planar UV completion shown in Figure 2.9, the roles of these symmetries
are exchanged. The U(1)N−1 symmetry is realized as a flavor symmetry that is preserved
by the planar superpotential, up to gauge transformations. The SU(N) global symme-
try, on the other hand, emerges in the infrared from the enhancement of the topological
symmetries associated with the gauge nodes.
We recall that the presence of the monopole superpotential Wmonopole plays a crucial role
in this enhancement mechanism. In particular, Wmonopole breaks the U(1)k topological
symmetries associated with a column containing k gauge nodes down to a single diagonal
U(1). As a result, each column of gauge nodes effectively contributes one independent
topological U(1) symmetry. The enhancement of these remaining topological symmetries
in the infrared gives rise to the non-Abelian SU(N) global symmetry of the G[U(N)]
SCFT.
Let us now move to the operator map. The chiral ring of the planar G[U(N)] theory
shown in Figure 2.9 is generated by the N−1 vertical bifundamental chiral fields connecting
adjacent vertical flavor nodes. Up to F-term relations imposed by the planar superpotential
Wplanar, these fields can be taken as the only independent generators of the chiral ring.
We expect these operators to map, under the duality, to monopole operators in the chiral
G[U(N)] theory in Figure 2.8. This expectation is correct, with the important caveat
that the fundamental monopole operators are gauge variant and must be appropriately
dressed by powers of fundamental chiral multiplets in order to construct gauge-invariant
operators.
To illustrate this, let us focus on a local segment of the chiral G[U(N)] quiver,

In this way, one constructs N −1 dressed fundamental monopole operators, one for each

We now verify that these operators have the correct quantum numbers to be mapped to

Starting from the equality of the partition functions of pairs of N = 4 mirror theories is

Lightning Recap: The N = 4 Dualization Algorithm
The dualization algorithm for N = 4 linear quiver theories provides a purely field-
theoretical proof of mirror duality, assuming only basic Seiberg-like dualities, and allows
us to access the full map of symmetries and protected gauge invariant operators.
It is well known that N = 4 linear quiver theories can be realized on Hanany-Witten brane
set-ups and inherit mirror dualities from the S-duality of Type IIB set-ups [19]. The idea
of the algorithm originates from the observation [30, 194, 195] that on linear or circular
brane setups, S−duality can act locally on each 5-brane creating an S−duality wall on its
right and an S−1-duality wall on its left: D5 = S·NS5·S−1 and NS5 = S·D5·S−1. The
dualization algorithm implements this local action of S-duality in field theory.
The dualization algorithm is based on two fundamental ingredients:
1. a realization of the generator S of the duality group SL(2; Z) that satisfies the fusion
to identity SS−1 = I. For N = 4 theories, this can be defined from the FT[U(N)]
SCFT [188]:

Figure 2.10: Applying the N = 4 dualization to U(N) SQCD with [F + 2N, F + 2N]
flavors proceeds as follows - in the first line, the quiver is broken into its constituent QFT
blocks and each block is sandwiched between an S−1 and S-wall. We have explicitly kept
the integral and integrating measures to remind the reader that care must be taken when
the gauge nodes are ungauged to implement the algorithm. The resulting QFT blocks
obtained after the local action are written in the second line, and are glued back together
by exploiting the fusion to identity property of the S-wall to obtain the final theory in the
third line. At this stage, an explicit Lagrangian description may be written using the UV
completion of the FT[U(N)] theory, which is indeed the mirror description one would get
by utilizing conventional HW brane setups. Note that we would need to incorporate HW
transitions to dualize more general quiver theories.
We point out that this move is slightly different than naively sandwiching the pre-
vious move between an S−1 and S wall. Observe that the gluing on the RHS does
not include the addition of U(N) adjoint multiplets coupling linearly to the moment
map of the glued FT[U(N)] theory.
We consider the case of N = 4 U(N) SQCD with F + 2N flavors to demonstrate the
algorithm at work (Figure 2.10).
When decomposing the theory into QFT blocks, it is important to keep in mind that each
block effectively plays the role of a matrix element, with two sets of indices corresponding
to the two N-dimensional vectors of parameters associated with the two U(N) global
symmetries. In particular, note that flavor blocks are accompanied by an identity wall,
reflecting this matrix structure.
It was shown that, armed with the fundamental blocks and duality moves described above,
one can construct the mirror of a vast set of N = 4 Lagrangian theories [187, 188, 196]. In
[26], the local dualization algorithm paradigm has been extended to a class of generalized
N = 2 quiver theories that can be realized in set-ups involving NS, NS′, D5, D5 branes
preserving four supercharges. Unlike the N = 4 case, where brane setups directly inform
the structure of the low-energy field theory, here the situation is reversed: the algorithmic
field theory approach yields an exact dual description, while the corresponding brane
configurations are difficult to interpret directly.
This inversion of logic highlights the
power of the algorithmic method — not only does it provide precise duals, but it also
offers a new lens through which to understand and possibly reconstruct the brane setups
themselves. Motivated by this, our aim is now to develop a similar algorithm tailored to
chiral-planar N = 2 theories in the remainder of this section.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
The N = 2 Toolbox
We now extend the N = 4 framework to N = 2 by considering appropriate real mass
deformations of the N = 4 basic blocks and corresponding identities here.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
Some key observations are in order:
– The contribution of the vector multiplet of the U(N) symmetry has disap-
peared, in agreement with the Higgsing of the U(N) →U(1)N.

reduces to a string of chiral multiplets connecting adjacent U(1) nodes.
– The quadratic form H[1N](⃗Z) encodes the CS levels and the BF couplings and

The Algorithm At Work
We are finally ready to define a dualization algorithm to dualize N = 2 quiver gauge
theories.
Along the lines of [188], we define the steps underlying the algorithmic dualization of an
N = 2 quiver:
• Step 1: The quiver is chopped into its basic QFT blocks that can be either bifun-
damentals or (anti-)fundamental chiral fields. This step is performed by freezing the
gauge interactions that will be reintroduced later.
• Step 2: Each QFT block is dualized via the basic duality moves described. These
identities, or basic moves are summarized for reference in Table 2.124.
• Step 3: The dualized blocks are glued together via the N = 2 chiral/planar fusion
to identity when the original gauging is restored.
Each of these steps will be illustrated in more detail in the following sections with explicit
examples. We reiterate that the choice of CS levels is not arbitrary - it is completely
fixed by our N = 4 parent.
We will use the algorithm to dualize non-Abelian chiral N = 2 quivers into abelian-planar
quivers. Although the same algorithm can be applied in reverse — starting from a planar
abelian quiver to obtain its chiral dual — we do not discuss this case here.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
The global symmetry of the SQCD theory is, in general,
S[U(F1 + N) × U(F2 + N)] × U(1),
where the first factor is the flavor symmetry acting on the fundamental and anti-fundamental
fields, while the second factor is the topological symmetry.11 In the planar mirror dual,
after taking into account gauge transformations and superpotential constraints, the only
manifest flavor symmetry is a single U(1).
The monopole superpotential Wmonopole contains terms that are linear in monopole oper-
ators carrying magnetic flux −1 and +1 under pairs of gauge nodes connected by vertical
links. These terms explicitly break the individual topological symmetries associated with
the nodes in each column down to a single diagonal U(1). As a result, the UV topological
symmetry of the planar quiver is
U(1)F1+F2+N−1,
which enhances in the IR to
S[U(F1 + N) × U(F2 + N)] .
This enhancement ensures that the global symmetry of the mirror quiver matches that of
the SQCD theory.
In the SQCD theory, the chiral ring is generated by mesonic operators constructed from the
fundamental fields Qi and anti-fundamentals ˜Qj, which transform in the bifundamental
representation of
SU(F1 + N) × SU(F2 + N).
Under the duality, these mesons are mapped to gauge-invariant monopole operators in the
planar Abelian theory. For instance, the meson QF1+N ˜QF2+N is mapped to the monopole
operator with magnetic flux +1 under the topmost gauge node of the Abelian quiver.
More generally, the remaining mesons are mapped to monopole operators characterized
by two strings of +1 magnetic fluxes, originating at the topmost node and propagating
along the two upper diagonals of the planar quiver. There are precisely (F1 + N)(F2 + N)
such monopole operators, reproducing the bifundamental representation of the enhanced
SU(F1 + N) × SU(F2 + N) global symmetry.
Finally, we note that in this class of dualities, the planar Abelian theory does not exhibit
any mesonic chiral ring generators. This implies that, for all values of F1 and F2, the
SQCD theory does not admit dressed gauge-invariant monopole operators that would
parametrize a non-compact moduli space.

The salient features of this duality closely parallel those of the duality shown in Figure 2.14.
As a result, the mapping between global symmetries and chiral ring generators proceeds
in direct analogy with the discussion presented at the end of Section 2.4.1.12
There is, however, an important qualitative difference. In this case, the planar dual can
admit an additional chiral ring generator, which from the point of view of the planar
theory is a mesonic operator. One can show that such an operator exists if and only if
F2 < N. Under the duality, this operator is mapped to a gauge-invariant dressed monopole
operator in the SQCD, carrying positive magnetic charge. This correspondence can be
explicitly checked, for instance by expanding the superconformal index, which confirms
that the same operator appears in the SQCD precisely for the same range F2 < N.

Chapter 2
An Example: The Planar Limit of “Electric” CS-SQCD3
In Section 2.1, we started from the N = 4 mirror pair of the U(N) SQCD and considered
the real mass deformation leading to the N = 2 mirror duality relating the chiral U(N)
SQCD to its planar quiver dual pair, depicted in Figure 2.2. A natural question is whether
we could exchange the two operations, i.e.: if we could take the planar limit of the SQCD
theory.
It is possible to directly take the planar limit of the SQCD theory, and follow the real
mass and Higgsing at the level of the S3
b partition function. However, since these quivers
are (generically) overbalanced, this is a tedious exercise for general values of N and F.
The dualization algorithm allows us to reach the planar limit of such theories and is the
subject of this section. To achieve this, we consider the chiral limit of the mirror of SQCD,

Chapter 2
By contrast, the appearance of the multiple U(2) factors is a genuinely new feature of this
duality. This enhancement involves topological symmetries associated with non-adjacent
gauge nodes and is therefore intrinsically non-local, unlike the local mechanism responsible
for the U(F −1) enhancement.
Remarkably, both types of enhancement are simultaneously realized and made manifest
in the planar dual description.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
Going Beyond the Algorithm
The results of this chapter strongly suggest that planar Abelian dual descriptions exist for
a broad class of three-dimensional N = 2 quiver gauge theories. While we do not provide
a formal proof that all N = 2 quivers admit such a description, our findings indicate that
this possibility is far more general than might have been expected a priori.
In particular, the strategy illustrated in Section 2.1—based on real mass deformations of
known N = 4 mirror pairs—appears to be applicable whenever the parent N = 4 theories
admit a Lagrangian description. In all cases analyzed, this procedure leads to a consistent
N = 2 chiral-planar mirror duality. 13
From this perspective, the assumptions required to implement the algorithm in the exam-
ples studied in this work should be viewed as primarily technical rather than fundamental.
Indeed, many of these restrictions can already be relaxed by performing a direct real mass
analysis, as in Section 2.1, or may be overcome by developing a more refined version of
the algorithm.
All examples presented in this work are derived from known Lagrangian N = 4 mirror
dualities and rely on a controlled real mass deformation analysis.
Nevertheless, many
additional generalizations appear possible and remain to be explored.
In closing, it is worth emphasizing that several of the remaining limitations of the present
algorithm may be addressed by extending it with additional moves already known in the
N = 4 mirror dualization framework.
Implementing the appropriate supersymmetry-
breaking deformations of these moves could lead to a significantly more powerful and
systematic construction of planar abelian duals for general N = 2 quiver gauge theories.
13This argument relies on the availability of a Lagrangian description of the N = 4 mirror theories. For
many interesting N = 4 SCFTs such a description is not known, and in those cases neither the partition
function nor the equations of motion are under sufficient control to directly apply the present strategy.

Chapter 3
The Zoology of N = 2 Unitary
CS-SQCD3 and their Planar
Abelian Duals
In Chapter 2 (based on [2, 3]), we showed that a large class of N = 2 Chern-Simons-matter
theories admit planar, Abelian dual descriptions. These planar Abelian duals arise from
suitable axial mass deformations of known N = 4 mirror pairs [19, 22]. Because they
descend from N = 4 theories, these N = 2 duals exist only on a special locus of parameter
space — the N = 4 descendant line
F = 2|k| + 2N
where the Chern–Simons level k is fixed by the number of flavors F and gauge rank N
of the parent theory. Away from this locus, the parameters (F, k, N) vary independently,
and the structure of a planar Abelian dual is, a priori, unclear. Extending the paradigm
of planar Abelianization beyond the N = 4 locus is, therefore, essential for obtaining a
unified understanding of the landscape of N = 2 SQCD.
The key observation of [5] is that real mass deformations provide a systematic
bridge between the known planar duals on the N = 4 descendant line and generic N = 2
theories. Real masses shift Chern–Simons levels, integrate out matter fields, and move
the theory through the (F, k, N) parameter space along controlled RG-flow trajectories.
By tracking these deformations simultaneously in the non-Abelian theory and its planar
mirror, we are able to study the duality away from the N = 4 locus and construct the
planar Abelian dual for any U(N)k1 SQCD with F fundamental fields.
The Taxonomy of N = 2 CS-SQCD3 with Fundamental
Matter
Three-dimensional N = 2 U(N)k SQCD theories with F fundamental chiral multiplets
can be organized in a two-dimensional parameter space spanned by (k, F). Each point in
this plane corresponds to a distinct theory. While we exclusively focus on k ≤0, analogous
generalizations can be seen for k > 0.
1While the subject of Chapter 2 are U(N)(k,k+Nℓ) theories, we focus on U(N)k here instead to connect
our findings to known Aharony-like dualities more transparently. The results obtained for U(N)k can be
generalized to theories with ℓ̸= 0 using the standard gauging/ungauging prescription.

Mapping Mass Flows Across Mirror Duals
In this section, we analyze how real-mass deformations of N = 2 SQCD are realized on
their planar Abelian duals. We do this by comparing the equations of motion on the
electric and mirror sides in the large-mass limit. We then focus on a pedagogical example
to elaborate on some features of our proposal. At each stage, we indicate the zone of the
k/F-plane (Figure 3.1) to which the theory belongs.

As a starting point, we consider the planar Abelian dual of U(N)N−F

we would obtain directly from N = 4 mirror symmetry.

Such theories lie in the maximally chiral regime, where all four deformations ±m and
±mH are available. Across the duality, a real mass for a quark in SQCD maps to an FI
term for the gauge nodes contained in a single column of the planar Abelian quiver, while
the FI parameter of the SQCD maps to a real mass for one of the two flavor nodes of the
Abelian theory.
Consequently, giving a real mass to the i-th quark corresponds to turning on a large FI
parameter for one (or two) adjacent columns of the Abelian quiver. Although different
choices of i are equivalent at the level of the IR-SCFT—being related by Weyl transforma-
tions of the SU(F) flavor symmetry—this symmetry is not manifest in the planar quiver
representation. As a result, the UV analysis of the deformation depends on the specific
choice of i, even though the corresponding IR deformations describe the same flow.
For concreteness, we adopt the following conventions for the four possible deformations:

a nontrivial self-consistency check of the duality originally proposed in [2, 3].

to different planar structures.
Successive applications of these deformations require a

the planar Abelian duals of SQCD theories obtained through multiple -m or +mH mass

Starting from this fully specified duality, including all background terms, one can in prin-
ciple invert the logic of our construction: by tracking the RG flow at the level of the S3
b
partition function, one can recover the N = 4 parent theory a la [5]. In the thesis, we
follow the opposite route—starting from the N = 4 descendants and performing deforma-
tions to obtain the general planar Abelian duals—but this observation highlights that the
k = 0 duality contains all the necessary data for the reverse reconstruction. Moreover, the
partition function provides an independent consistency check for our proposal and allows
us to recover results for N = 4 descendants.
We point out that the planar duals of theories in Zones 1, ˜1 naturally encode Aharony-like
duality, rather than being derived from it. In these regions of parameter space, a theory
generically admits a planar dual whose quiver has dimensions N × (F −N), manifestly
symmetric under the exchange N ↔F −N.
As a consequence, theories related by Aharony-like duality flow to the same planar de-
scription. For instance, the Aharony-like dual pair consisting of U(3)−1
SQCD with 7
fundamental chiral multiplets and U(4)1
SQCD with 7 antifundamental chiral multiplets
gives rise to an identical planar dual, shown in Equation 3.57. We expand on this in
Section 3.4.

Chapter 3
Relating Planar Abelian Duals and Aharony-like Duals
The N = 2 U(N)k SQCD theory enjoys an Aharony-like duality for arbitrary parameters
(N, k, F) (see [127] and references therein). In this section, we restrict to theories with
only fundamental matter fields, whose dual descriptions involve only anti-fundamental
fields. As discussed previously, the structure of these dualities depends qualitatively on
the region of the (k, F) plane at fixed N (see Figure 3.37).
Our goal is to clarify how the planar Abelian dualities proposed in this chapter relate to
Aharony duality. We argue that, assuming the validity of the planar Abelian dualities
together with certain special instances of Aharony-like duality—namely those involving
Abelian gauge groups in both the electric and magnetic phases (see Appendix B)—one
can derive the general Aharony-like dualities for U(N)k SQCD with F fundamental chiral
fields. In this sense, Aharony duality emerges from more elementary building blocks and
is effectively proven within this framework. An immediate consequence is that Aharony
duality for SQCD with fundamentals can be understood as a consequence of mirror sym-
metry.
To make this concrete, we consider SQCD in Zone ˜1 with F > 2N fundamentals and
Chern–Simons level k, satisfying 2|k| < 2N −F, corresponding to a maximally chiral
regime [21]. This theory is Aharony-dual to U(F −N) SQCD with F anti-fundamentals
and Chern–Simons level −k, which lies in (the anti-fundamental analogue of) Zone 1:
U(N)k with F < 2N □

Across the duality, the topological symmetry is mapped to a linear combination of the
R-symmetry and minus the topological symmetry of the magnetic gauge group:
U(1)top = k U(1)R −U(1)top .
(3.60)
We now show that the planar Abelian duals of these SQCD theories—referred to as the
electric and magnetic theories—are equivalent up to a sequence of local dualities. These
local dualities relate SQED theories to SQED theories and can be understood as limiting
cases of mirror symmetry or Aharony duality. Assuming the validity of this local du-
alization procedure, it follows that Aharony-like dualities for SQCD with fundamentals
arise as a consequence of mirror symmetry. We summarize this structure schematically in
Figure 3.10.

Chapter 3
Electric Theory.
We begin by rewriting the planar Abelian dual of the electric theory
(3.43), characterized by gauge group U(N), Chern–Simons level k, FI parameter η, and
trial R-charge r. We then perform a sequence of dualizations analogous to the planar
flip–flip duality discussed in [3], referring the reader to that work for the details of the
individual steps.
The dualization proceeds column by column, starting from the rightmost column and
stopping at the column immediately to the right of the bottom flavor node. The result of
this first pass is shown in the central quiver of Figure 3.11. After this step, a column of
square nodes appears at position F
2 −|k|, the flavor node shifts one column to the right,
and the bottom-right diagonal shortens by one node.
We then repeat this procedure iteratively. Each pass starts from the right and ends at the
column adjacent to the bottom flavor node, shortening the bottom-right diagonal by one
node and reducing the number of columns involved by one. The result after the second
pass is shown on the right of Figure 3.11.
After a finite number of iterations, we arrive at the quiver shown on the left of Figure 3.12,
which has the form of a rectangular lattice of size N ×(F −N). Finally, we apply a flip–flip
duality [3] to the rightmost triangular subquiver, obtaining the quiver displayed on the
right of Figure 3.12.
This provides our final expression for the electric theory and can be viewed as an alternative
presentation of the planar Abelian dual in Zone ˜1.

The final step consists of manipulating the two chiral fields connected to the flavor nodes.
This is achieved by performing a sequence of Witten’s SL(2; Z) transformations [87] acting
on the U(1)η symmetry of the quiver, which combine to an overall trivial action. The non-
trivial effect arises from the fact that there is a single flavor U(1) symmetry in the planar
mirror, which can be associated with either the left or the right flavor node; these two
choices are gauge equivalent.
Concretely, we perform a T −1ST −1 transformation on the left flavor node and a T S−1T
transformation on the right flavor node. Although these combine into a trivial SL(2; Z)
action, the intermediate steps induce a non-trivial reorganization of the theory, which
remains IR dual to the original quiver. The T transformation shifts the Chern–Simons
level by +1, while the S transformation gauges the symmetry and introduces a new U(1)
topological symmetry6.

This concludes the argument that Aharony duality for U(N)k SQCD with F fundamental

We expect that a similar argument extends to more general U(N)k theories
with both fundamental and anti-fundamental matter, although we leave this extension for

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
Scope and Future Directions
In this chapter, we have restricted our attention to U(N)k CS–SQCD with F fundamen-
tal chiral multiplets. We conclude by briefly commenting on natural extensions of this
analysis.
Real Mass Deformations of CS-SQCD3 with both Fundamental and Anti-
Fundamental Fields.
Planar Abelian duals were originally derived for theories contain-
ing only a single type of chiral multiplet—either all fundamentals or all anti-fundamentals.
Planar duals for CS-SQCD3 with both F fundamental and A anti-fundamental fields can
also be constructed, though a full treatment of such general chiral configurations lies be-
yond the scope of this chapter. Nevertheless, real mass deformations provide a systematic
bridge from the N = 4 descendant locus to generic N = 2 theories with asymmetric
matter content.
A natural starting point for analyzing these theories is the class of U(N)k SQCD models
with [F, F] flavors satisfying the constraint 2|k| = 2F −2N. As an illustrative exam-
ple, consider U(2)(−1,−3) SQCD with [3, 3] chiral multiplets, which arises as an N = 4
descendant. Its planar mirror dual can be constructed via the chiral-planar dualization
algorithm (see Equation 2.14 and the accompanying discussion), or by adding suitable
N = 4 supersymmetry-breaking real-mass deformations. Starting from this theory, one
can explore the full three-dimensional parameter space (k, F, A) by turning on real mass
deformations and tracking their effects in the planar Abelian dual. We emphasize that all
these dualities can be viewed, in a broad sense, as instances of mirror symmetry.
We now turn on real mass deformations for selected chiral multiplets (see Figure 3.14 for
a summary):
• Assigning positive real masses, +m, to two of the fundamental chirals and integrat-
ing them out shifts the Chern–Simons levels appropriately. The theory then flows
to U(2)(0,−2) with [1, 3] chirals. Although this configuration lies outside the direct
domain of the dualization algorithm, its planar dual can be systematically deter-
mined by following the controlled RG flow. The corresponding trajectory is shown
in Equation 3.68.
• Conversely, assigning negative real masses, −m, to two fundamental chirals leads to
a distinct RG-flow trajectory, depicted in Equation 3.69. Again, the resulting theory
falls outside the original algorithmic regime but remains accessible through the same
deformation logic.
• We can also assign a positive real mass +m to one fundamental chiral and a negative
real mass −m to a second chiral.
The resulting RG flow trajectory is shown in
Equation 3.70.
Again, the resulting theory falls outside the original algorithmic
regime but remains accessible through the same deformation logic.
• One can also turn on a negative mass −mF for one fundamental and a positive mass
+mA for one anti-fundamental. The resulting RG flow is shown in Equation 3.71.
Again, the resulting theory falls outside the original algorithmic regime but remains
accessible through the same deformation logic.
• Similarly, a positive mass +mF for one fundamental together with a negative mass
−mA for one anti-fundamental produces the trajectory in Equation 3.72. This con-
figuration is in the domain of the dualization algorithm and provides a non-trivial
consistency check of this paradigm.

Chapter 3
• One can also turn on a negative mass −mF for one fundamental and a negative mass
−mA for one anti-fundamental. The resulting RG flow is shown in Equation 3.73.
Again, the resulting theory falls outside the original algorithmic regime but remains
accessible through the same deformation logic.
• Similarly, one can also turn on a positive mass +mF for one fundamental and a
positive mass +mA for one anti-fundamental. The resulting RG flow is shown in
Equation 3.74.
Again, the resulting theory falls outside the original algorithmic
regime but remains accessible through the same deformation logic. Notice the qual-
itative similarity in the resulting theory with the one obtained in Equation 3.73 –
this is in line with our expectations.
To emphasize the qualitative differences in the RG flows induced by the same mass de-
formations for different values of ℓ, we also study the effect of the same on U(2)−1 SQCD
with [3, 3] chiral multiplets in Figure 3.15.
We can recover the results of Figure 3.14
at each step consistently by following the gauging/ungauging prescription. Notice that
SQCD theories with |F −A| = 2|k| are marginally chiral and possess an additional gauge
invariant monopole that parametrizes a C-moduli space of vacua. Consistently, the planar
dual of these theories possesses an additional gauge-invariant mesonic operator.
These examples illustrate how real mass deformations enlarge the effective domain of
planar Abelianization, allowing one to probe chiral CS-SQCD3 theories beyond the N = 4
descendant locus. Their taxonomy, however, is more involved, and is deferred to future
work.
Dualities for TQFTs.
Furthermore, the RG-flow trajectories we analyze naturally yield
planar dual descriptions for pure U(N)k supersymmetric Yang–Mills theories. It would
be interesting to investigate these dualities in greater depth, particularly through the lens
of generalized global symmetries and their associated topological operators.

Part III: N = 2 Mirror Symmetry
in Symplectic Gauge Theories

Chapter 4
Mirror Duals of N = 2 Symplectic
Chern-Simons-Matter Theories
The constructions of Chapters 2 and 3 (based on [2, 3]) focused on theories with unitary
gauge groups. In this chapter, we extend the paradigm of planar Abelianization to theories
with symplectic gauge symmetries. Three-dimensional N = 4 symplectic gauge theories
are distinguished by the existence of two distinct mirror descriptions.
These mirrors
typically take the form of either unitary D-type quiver gauge theories [154, 203–206]
or quivers involving orthosymplectic gauge groups [207], which can be engineered via
orientifolding Hanany–Witten brane setups.
While both classes of mirrors capture the same infrared physics [208, 209], incorporating
orthosymplectic quivers within the paradigm of planar Abelianization presents a signifi-
cant challenge. In such descriptions, not all properties of the infrared SCFT are manifest
in the ultraviolet Lagrangian, notably hidden FI parameters and the associated symme-
try enhancement [125, 205, 210].
By contrast, unitary D-type quivers provide a fully
Lagrangian and transparent framework in which these issues do not arise.
Accordingly, in this chapter (based on [6]), we focus on planar Abelian duals of USp gauge
theories, obtained from N = 4 USp SQCD and their unitary D-type quiver mirrors. This
setting allows us to construct explicit N = 2 Abelian mirror duals for symplectic gauge
theories and to extend the paradigm of planar Abelianization beyond the unitary case in
a controlled and systematic manner.
Planar Abelian Duals of U(2N) CS-SQCD3 with a
Tensor
We begin from a somewhat unconventional vantage point: the mirror dual of an N = 4
U(2N) gauge theory with F hypermultiplets and a rank-2 antisymmetric tensor [206, 211,
212]. This setup provides a natural and instructive arena in which to explore how the
paradigm of planar Abelianization extends to gauge theories with tensor matter.
Beyond its intrinsic interest, this theory furnishes a nontrivial consistency check across dif-
ferent gauge groups. Indeed, both unitary and symplectic theories can be recovered
as appropriate limits of this construction: the former by integrating out the antisymmetric

Although both theories preserve N = 4 supersymmetry, it is convenient to describe them

Mapping Gauge-Invariant Operators.
Mirror symmetry provides a precise map be-
tween the order and disorder operators of the two theories:
• The fundamental monopoles M± of the electric SQCD theory are mapped to the
mesonic operators in the mirror theory constructed by contracting the fields con-
necting the two U(1) flavor nodes.
• The composite operator λ ˜Q ˜Q (and its conjugate ˜λQQ) are mapped to a collection
of monopole operators of the mirror theory. More precisely, these monopoles carry
GNO fluxes ∓1 under consecutive gauge nodes starting from the gauge node with
topological symmetry U(1)−XF −XF −1+s.
The Flow to the N = 2 Chiral-Planar Mirror Duals
We now consider a supersymmetry-breaking mass deformation, implemented by turning
on a nonzero vacuum expectation value for the real scalar in the background vector mul-
tiplet associated with the U(1)τ symmetry. Since U(1)τ is a flavor subgroup of the N = 4
R-symmetry, this deformation breaks SU(2)C × SU(2)H →U(1)R and reduces supersym-
metry from N = 4 to N = 2.
For generic values of the Coulomb branch parameters, the infrared theory is a topological
quantum field theory.
However, there exist distinguished loci on the Coulomb branch
where the flow instead leads to an interacting N = 2 theory. The identification of these
vacua and the resulting effective descriptions can be carried out efficiently at the level of

the S3
b partition function, following the methods developed in [2, 3]. We do not repeat
these analyses and limit ourselves to a qualitative summary of the outcome.
We first consider the “electric” N = 4 SQCD theory. We move on the Coulomb branch
in such a way to reach an interacting vacuum where all the fundamental chiral fields
Q and the anti-chiral component of the antisymmetric tensor ˜λ remain massless, while
the adjoint Φ, the F anti-chiral fields ˜Q and the chiral component of the antisymmetric
tensor λ acquire a mass and are integrated out. Integrating out these fields generates
CS interactions, resulting in an N = 2 U(2N) theory with F fundamentals Q and an
anti-chiral antisymmetric field ˜λ with CS level fixed by the matter content

In direct analogy with the analysis of SQCD without tensor matter, the corresponding

in the chiral ring of the electric theory.

generic GNO flux assignment shown in Equation 4.3. These monopoles also have

This case is particularly rich, as the chiral ring is generated by a larger class of operators,
including composite mesons of the form ˜λ Q Q, baryonic operators Q2N, and composite

The study of this duality is, however, complicated by an ambiguity in gauging the U(1)

Chapter 4
flavor symmetries on the mirror side: one may choose to gauge either of the two U(1)
factors, leading to inequivalent presentations of the gauge-invariant monopole operators.
In particular, one choice yields a description in which the composite operators (˜λ˜λ)k
are mapped to monopoles with higher GNO flux, whereas the alternative choice leads
to a presentation in which the composite mesons and baryonic operators are mapped to
monopoles with higher GNO flux. As such, we defer the resolution of this ambiguity and
a more detailed study of these theories to future work.
A Dual for U(2N)(0,−3N) CS-SQCD3 with F ≥2N + 2 □and
We conclude by proposing an Abelian quiver gauge theory that is infrared dual to U(2N)(0,−3N)
SQCD with F fundamental chiral multiplets and a rank-2 antisymmetric tensor, with
F ≥2N + 2 in Equation 4.4. Consistency of Chern–Simons level quantization requires F
to be even.
This proposal is motivated by three complementary considerations. First, its structure
closely parallels the planar Abelian duals of U(N)0 SQCD with F fundamental chiral
multiplets proposed in [5]. Second, we have verified agreement of superconformal indices
for several low-rank and low-flavor examples, providing nontrivial evidence in favor of
the duality.
Finally, under appropriate real mass deformations, the proposed Abelian
quiver flows to the known N = 4 descendant theories discussed before. A more detailed a
posteriori justification of this proposal will be given once we construct the analogous dual
theory for USp(2N)0 with F fundamental multiplets (see the discussion before Equation
4.47), since both constructions share many qualitative features.

Planar Abelian Duals of Symplectic CS-SQCD3
Three-dimensional N = 4 gauge theories with symplectic gauge group are known to admit
two distinct mirror descriptions. The first is a unitary quiver with the structure of a D-type
Dynkin diagram (which we will refer to as a D-type quiver) [154, 203–206]. The second
description is a linear quiver with alternating orthogonal and symplectic gauge groups
[207], which can be obtained from Hanany–Witten brane constructions in the presence of
O3 planes (often denoted Feng-Hanany setups).
In this work, we restrict our attention to the first mirror dual, postponing the analysis of
orthosymplectic mirror duals to future work. The D-type mirror dual of USp(2N) SQCD
is shown in Figure 4.2, while the orthosymplectic mirror dual is displayed for completeness
in Figure 4.3.

We begin by analyzing the chiral limit of the SQCD theory. More precisely, we perform
a supersymmetry-breaking deformation from N = 4 to N = 2 in USp(2N) SQCD that
preserves the gauge group. At the level of the S3
b partition function, this deformation
follows the same steps outlined in Section 2.1 ([3]). The adjoint and the F chiral multiplets3
acquire a mass and are integrated out, generating a Chern–Simons level equal to 2N+2−F.
The resulting theory is an N = 2 gauge theory with gauge group USp(2N)2N+2−F and F
chiral multiplets in the fundamental representation.
The deformation can be mapped to the mirror side of Figure 4.2, yielding the planar
Abelian quiver shown in Figure 4.4. This mapping admits an algorithmic generalization
closely analogous to that of [3], as further discussed in [214].

We conclude with a few remarks highlighting the main differences between the planar dual
of USp(2N) and the planar dual of U(N) shown in Section 2.1:
• There is no flavor node attached to the topmost gauge node. As a consequence, no
long mesons can be formed on the planar side, which in turn implies the absence
of gauge-invariant monopole operators on the USp(2N) side. The sole exception to
this is encountered for F = 2N + 2 fundamental multiplets, which we discuss via an
example in Section 4.4.3.
• In the last column, the full FI parameter of the topmost gauge node is −XF −XF−1+
4iQ(1−r). The second-highest node has FI parameter XF −XF−1, while the third-
highest node has FI parameter −XF −XF−1 + iQ(1 −r). The fourth-highest node
again has FI parameter XF −XF−1, and this pattern continues down the column.

Planar Abelian Duals of DF Quiver Gauge Theories
The second deformation of the duality in Figure 4.2 is the planar limit of the SQCD theory,
which corresponds to a chiral limit of the D-type mirror. This deformation preserves the
non-abelian structure of all gauge nodes in the mirror theory. As a result, one obtains an
N = 2 D-type unitary quiver on the right-hand side and a planar abelian quiver on the
left-hand side.
Because the resulting N = 2 electric theory exhibits several peculiar features, it is useful
to first analyze an explicit example before presenting the general proposal.

Although different chiral limits may be realized on the planar side, depending on

choices.

Similarly to the USp(2) case, there is an “orthogonal” superpotential Worth that preserves

Both of these superpotentials are remnants of the

accordingly, ensuring that all topological symmetries in the electric theory are broken.
We emphasize that multiple chiral limits of the mirror theory are possible, depending on
which fundamental and anti-fundamental fields acquire a mass and are integrated out.
These correspond to analogous deformations on the electric side that Higgs the gauge
group. Importantly, the resulting symmetry-breaking pattern is independent of the specific
fields that survive the deformation.
Mapping Mass Flows Across Mirror Duals
The planar Abelian duals of symplectic SQCD constructed in Section 4.2 arise from specific
axial mass deformations of known N = 4 mirror pairs. Since these N = 2 duals descend

Chapter 4
from N = 4 theories, they exist only for a restricted range of parameters:
F = 2N + 2 + |k|,
where the CS level k is fixed in terms of the rank and flavor content of the N = 4 parent.
For values of (F, k, N) that do not satisfy this relation, it is not a priori guaranteed that
the dual description will remain a planar Abelian quiver. Supersymmetry is broken for
F < 2N + 2 −|k|;
this bound coincides with the regime in which the Aharony-like dual gauge group has a
negative rank, signaling the absence of a supersymmetric vacuum [21].
The key observation of [5] is that real mass deformations provide a systematic
bridge between N = 4 descendants and more general N = 2 theories. Real masses shift
Chern–Simons levels, integrate out matter fields, and move the theory along controlled
RG-flow trajectories in the (F, k, N) parameter space. By tracking these deformations
simultaneously in the non-Abelian theory and its planar mirror, we can follow how the
quiver structure, CS interactions, and monopole superpotentials evolve, thereby extending
planar Abelian duality beyond the original N = 4 descendants.
Analogous to [5], we can organize N = 2 USp(2N)k SQCD3 with F chiral multiplets in a
two-dimensional parameter space spanned by (|k|, F). Each point in this plane corresponds
to a distinct theory. In this paper, we focus on SQCD with fundamentals, with CS level
k ≤0. The case k > 0 is analogous. We show this organization in Figure 4.7.

A Dual for USp(2N)0 CS-SQCD3 with F ≥2N + 2 □and its Defor-
mations
We begin by proposing an Abelian quiver gauge theory that is infrared dual to USp(2N)0
SQCD with F fundamental multiplets, where F ≥2N + 2. Consistency of Chern–Simons
level quantization requires F to be even.
This proposal is motivated by three complementary considerations. First, its structure
closely parallels the planar Abelian duals of U(N)0 SQCD with F fundamental chiral
multiplets proposed in [5]. Second, we have verified agreement of superconformal indices
for several low-rank and low-flavor examples, providing nontrivial evidence for the duality.
Finally, upon performing appropriate real mass deformations, the proposed Abelian quiver
correctly flows to the known N = 4 descendant theories discussed in earlier sections.

Mapping Multiple Negative Mass Deformations
Starting from the duality between USp(2N)0 SQCD with F fundamental multiplets and its
planar Abelian dual (see Equation 4.18), we now introduce negative real mass deformations
and study the corresponding RG flows in both descriptions.
On the “electric” side, this deformation drives the theory to USp(2N)−1 SQCD with F −1
fundamental multiplets. The analysis of the RG trajectory in the planar Abelian dual is
more subtle. We propose that the corresponding deformation is implemented by Higgsing
all the chiral fields along the bottom-left diagonal, while the chiral field connecting the
bottom-most gauge and flavor nodes acquires a positive mass. This Higgsing identifies
all gauge nodes along the diagonal with the final node, and the planar superpotential
correspondingly reduces to mass terms.

sequence 2N −1 times, the resulting quiver matches the planar Abelian dual of the N = 4
descendant SQCD.
Thus, our analysis of USp(2N) theories naturally sheds light on U(2N) gauge theories
with a rank-2 antisymmetric tensor. Understanding how to construct planar mirror duals
for USp(2N)k SQCD with arbitrary F naturally informs the corresponding construction
for U(2N)(˜k,˜k−3N) SQCD with F fundamentals and a rank-2 antisymmetric tensor. This
provides an a posteriori justification for our choice to begin from the U(2N) theory in
Section 4.1.

We have the possibility of studying the RG flow trajectories induced by both ±m defor-

an FI term for the gauge nodes contained in a single column of the planar Abelian quiver,
while the FI parameter of the SQCD maps to a real mass for one of the two flavor nodes
of the Abelian theory.
Consequently, giving a real mass to the i-th quark corresponds to turning on a large FI
parameter for one (or two) adjacent columns of the Abelian quiver. Although different
choices of i are equivalent at the level of the IR-SCFT—being related by Weyl transfor-
mations of the U(F) flavor symmetry—this symmetry is not manifest in the planar quiver
representation. As a result, the UV analysis of the deformation depends on the specific
choice of i, even though the corresponding IR deformations describe the same flow.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
A Subtlety along the RG Flow
Recall that the Aharony-like dual of electric USp(2N)k SQCD with F fundamental multi-
plets is a magnetic USp(2 ˜N)−k SQCD with F antifundamental multiplets, together with
�F

gauge-singlet chiral fields. In the region of parameter space defined by
(F < 2N + 2) ∩(−F + 2N + 2 = |k|),
the rank of the magnetic gauge group vanishes, ˜N = 0. As a consequence, the theory
s-confines in the deep infrared, and the low-energy spectrum consists solely of
�F

gauge-
singlet chiral multiplets.
This behavior is completely transparent in the Aharony-like
magnetic description.
In the context of Figure 4.10, this implies that USp(4)±1 SQCD with 5 fundamental
multiplets and USp(4)±2 SQCD with 4 fundamental multiplets should both s-confine. We
verify that this expectation is indeed borne out: the corresponding planar duals of these
theories can be made to s-confine through an appropriate sequence of local dualizations.
We point out that in the corresponding N = 4 parent theories, the deep infrared is
characterized by symmetry enhancement that is not visible in the UV Lagrangian [125,
208–210, 215]. By contrast, in the planar N = 2 theories obtained along the RG flow,
no such enhancement takes place: the Abelianized description already accounts for the
full infrared spectrum, including confinement and the correct counting of gauge-singlet
degrees of freedom.
Insights from Index Spectroscopy.
It is instructive to examine more closely the
superconformal index of the s-confining theories discussed above. The index of a theory
consisting of F free chiral multiplets has a particularly distinctive structure. Assigning a
trial R-charge r and a fugacity φi to each free field, the index takes the form

Beyond the vacuum contribution, the term of order xr arises from the bosonic primaries of
each chiral multiplet and corresponds to the character of the fundamental representation
of U(F).
The contribution at order x2−r originates from the fermionic primaries and
transforms in the antifundamental representation of U(F). Finally, the term of order x2
counts the conserved currents of the theory6 and correctly reproduces the character of the
adjoint representation of U(F).
The salient features of the superconformal index of a free theory can therefore be summa-
rized as follows:
• There is a bosonic positive contribution at order xr and a fermionic negative contri-
bution at order x2−r; these two terms transform in conjugate representations of the
flavor symmetry group.
• The contribution at order x2 is precisely given by the product of the first bosonic
and fermionic terms.
6The contribution at order x2 may in principle receive additional contributions from composite op-
erators; throughout this discussion, we assume that the trial R-charge r is chosen so as to avoid this
possibility.

Table 4.2: Values of the superconformal index for USp(4)±k SQCD3 with five chiral fields
are shown here. All fields are assigned a trial R-charge r, and φ denotes the fugacities of
the UV flavor symmetry group U(5). The notation Λn refers to the n-index antisymmetric
representation of U(5), while Λ
n denotes its complex conjugate.
These properties must persist even when the full infrared symmetry group is not manifest
from a ultraviolet perspective. This is precisely what occurs in s-confining theories. As
an illustrative example, consider the USp(4)1 theory with five chiral multiplets, which is
known to confine [21]. In the ultraviolet, the symmetry group is U(5), acting on the five
chiral fields, and the ten gauge-invariant mesons transform in the two-index antisymmetric
representation Λ2 of this group. In the infrared, however, the symmetry is enhanced to
U(10). Since the confined theory is free, the two structural properties of the index discussed
above must be realized, even though the index itself is written solely in terms of ultraviolet
fugacities.
The mechanism underlying this symmetry enhancement is highly non-trivial and relies on
delicate cancellations among different contributions to the superconformal index, as can
be verified by explicit computations. While the mesons and the 25 conserved currents
of U(5) are captured by the zero GNO flux sector, the fermionic contribution at order
−x2−2r, together with the remaining conserved currents, arises from the +1 GNO flux
sector7. Note that the tensor product Λ2 ⊗Λ
2 always contains the adjoint representation,
corresponding to the classical contribution to the current algebra.
It is worth emphasizing that it is quite remarkable that contributions from different GNO
flux sectors recombine to reproduce the product structure characteristic of a free theory.
This phenomenon results from the interplay between the matter and vector multiplet
contributions and the specific value of the Chern–Simons level that ensures confinement.
It is therefore natural to ask how this structure is modified as the Chern–Simons level is
varied. In Table 4.2, we report the values of the superconformal index for several choices
of the level k.
As we vary k, the rank of the representation appearing in the fermionic contribution
changes by one unit, while its R-charge shifts by 1 −r. Confinement is reached when
the representation becomes Λ
2. At this point, the coefficient of the x2 term factorizes
automatically, in agreement with the expected confining behavior.
Scope and Future Directions
In this chapter, we have restricted attention to USp(2N) CS-SQCD3 with fundamental
matter and their real mass deformations. Within this class, we were able to track how real
7The Weyl group of USp(2N) is (Z2)N ⋊SN, acting on the GNO flux vector (m1, . . . , mN) by permu-
tations and sign inversions. As a result, the +1 sector is uniquely characterized by the GNO configuration
(+1, 0, . . . , 0).

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
mass deformations shift the Chern–Simons levels and follow the induced RG flows in both
the electric and planar Abelian mirror descriptions. Understanding these RG flows also
allows us to track analogous flows in U(2N) CS-SQCD3 with fundamental matter and a
rank-2 antisymmetric tensor and their mirror duals, since these two families of theories
share many qualitative features.
A natural extension of this framework is to generalize the planar Abelianization prescrip-
tion to SO(N) CS–SQCD3. For N = 4 theories, these gauge groups can be realized as
Z2 quotients of a U(2N) gauge symmetry [150, 207], and we anticipate that a similar
mechanism operates in the N = 2 setting considered here. Another promising direction
is the investigation of Aharony duality [21] in the symplectic setting. In the unitary case,
[5] showed that Aharony duality can be realized as a consequence of mirror duality, and
it would be interesting to uncover how an analogous structure manifests in USp(2N) CS-
SQCD3. Finally, a further direction is to explore generalizations of planar Abelianization
to theories with more general tensor matter content.

Part IV: Non-Supersymmetric
Planar Mirror Symmetry

Chapter 5
Non-Supersymmetric Unitary
Dualities
Over the past fifteen years, the study of IR bosonization dualities in three-dimensional CS
gauge theories has become a vibrant and rapidly developing area of theoretical physics.
These dualities relate theories with fundamentally different microscopic degrees of freedom
—bosonic versus fermionic matter — yet identical long-distance physics. Beginning with
large-N analyses and later extending to finite-rank gauge groups, this program has led to
a detailed understanding of strongly coupled dynamics in three dimensions [34–42, 44].
A particularly fruitful idea has been to derive non-supersymmetric dualities as mass de-
formations of known supersymmetric ones [43, 216–220]. Thus, non-supersymmetric 3D
Chern-Simons dualities have become a central tenet within contemporary research on
QFTs, with broad implications across particle physics, condensed matter physics, and
mathematics.
In [4], we proposed a new class of dualities relating non-Abelian theories—such as SU(N)
Chern–Simons QCD3 with both bosonic and fermionic matter—to Abelian theories with
multiple U(1) gauge groups. A defining feature of the Abelian duals is that both gauge
and matter content are naturally encoded in planar quiver diagrams: scalar fields are
represented by blue links, fermions by red links, and each face of the planar embedding
corresponds to an interaction term in the effective action. These dualities closely parallel
recently established N = 2 mirror dualities1 [2, 3], which themselves descend from mass-
deformed N = 4 mirror pairs [19, 22].
A simple rule governs the quiver structure: adding a bosonic flavor on the QCD side
corresponds to inserting a fermionic column in the quiver (red), while adding a fermionic
flavor adds a bosonic column (blue). In this way, our dualities can be viewed as gener-
alizations of bosonization dualities [221–223], now extended to non-Abelian theories with
richer matter content.
We provide several nontrivial consistency checks of these dualities. First, we establish an
explicit operator map relating gauge-invariant mesons, baryons, and conserved currents
of the non-Abelian theory to appropriately dressed monopole operators in the Abelian
quiver. Second, we study mass deformations of individual bosonic and fermionic fields
1We expect these non-supersymmetric dualities to arise from supersymmetry-breaking mass deforma-
tions of the N = 2 dualities of [2, 3]. A detailed analysis of the RG flows connecting the supersymmetric
and non-supersymmetric theories lies beyond the scope of this chapter.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
and follow the resulting renormalization group flows on both sides of the duality. These
flows generate new dual pairs within the same framework and provide stringent tests of
the proposed correspondence.
Taken together, these results suggest that the dualities developed in this chapter may be
interpreted as a form of non-Abelian mirror symmetry without supersymmetry, extending
the conceptual reach of mirror symmetry beyond the supersymmetric setting 2.

In this section, we review a duality for Abelian Chern-Simons QED3 with both bosonic
and fermionic matter. This duality is a mild generalization of the bosonization dualities
originally discussed in [224], and serves as a useful starting point for the more general
non-Abelian constructions developed later in this chapter.

These are obtained by dressing the bare monopole M+, which carries gauge charge −Ns,

Sigma-model limits.
Further evidence for the duality arises from the behavior under
relevant deformations.
In the bosonic QED theory in Eq. (5.10), one may turn on a
negative mass squared for all scalars in a way that preserves the global SU(Ns) symmetry.
The scalars condense, Higgsing the gauge group and driving the theory to a CPNs−1
nonlinear sigma model in the infrared [192].
On the dual side, the corresponding deformation gives negative masses to the fermions,
leading to a (U(1)0)Ns−1 gauge theory.
We conjecture that the Ns −1 dual photons
combine with the Ns −1 real scalars σi to form a complex (Ns −1)-dimensional target
space, reproducing the expected sigma-model description on the original side.
Global Symmetries
We also discuss the global symmetry structure of the duality shown in Figure 5.1.
On the QED side, the continuous global symmetry is, up to discrete factors,
U(1)top × S
�U(Ns) × U(Nf)

,
where U(1)top is the topological symmetry associated with the Abelian gauge field, while
the special unitary factors act on the scalar and fermionic matter fields, respectively.
On the quiver side, the symmetry structure depends on the value of the parameter k. For
k > 0, the theory contains Nf + Ns independent U(1) topological symmetries, one for
each gauge node. When k = 0, one linear combination of these topological symmetries
is absent, leaving Nf + Ns −1 topological symmetries together with an additional U(1)
flavor symmetry rotating the matter fields. In both cases, the total rank of the continuous
global symmetry matches that of the QED theory.
The duality therefore predicts a non-trivial infrared enhancement of symmetry on the
quiver side: although the UV description exhibits an Abelian U(1)Nf+Ns global symmetry,
this symmetry is expected to reorganize into the non-Abelian group
S
�U(Ns) × U(Nf)

in the infrared, in precise agreement with the global symmetry of the QED theory.
