Mass Deformations
Mass deformations of the matter fields in the QED theory admit a simple and intuitive
mapping to mass deformations in the dual quiver theory. Schematically, the correspon-
dence takes the form

In other words, scalar mass deformations on one side of the duality are mapped to fermionic
mass deformations on the other side, and vice versa, with a relative sign dictated by the
structure of the duality.
One can explicitly verify that turning on masses for the fermions and positive masses for
the scalars reproduces the same duality shown in Figure 5.1, albeit with different values
of the parameters k, Nf, and Ns. This behavior provides a non-trivial consistency check
of our proposal, as it confirms that the duality is stable under continuous deformations of
the microscopic parameters.
Turning on a negative mass for one of the scalars instead triggers a Higgs mechanism on
the QED side, partially breaking the gauge symmetry. On the dual side, the corresponding
deformation gives a positive mass to one of the fermions, which can then be integrated
out. This initiates a sequential confinement process along the quiver, ultimately resulting
in a theory of free fields. The infrared description obtained in this way precisely matches
that of the Higgsed electric theory, providing further support for the proposed duality.
Planar Abelianization in Bosonic CS-QCD3
In this section, we construct a quiver gauge theory dual to non-Abelian QCD3 with fun-
damental matter. Our proposal builds upon recent results [2–4] that established a duality
between N = 2 supersymmetric non-Abelian linear CS quiver gauge theories and planar
Abelian quiver gauge theories with mixed CS interactions. Motivated by these supersym-
metric dualities, we conjecture an analogous correspondence in the non-supersymmetric
case: non-Abelian CS QCD3 with fundamental fermionic and bosonic matter should be
dual to a planar Abelian quiver theory containing both bosons and fermions. This ex-
pectation follows from the observation that our proposed non-supersymmetric dualities
should emerge as mass deformations of their supersymmetric counterparts.

We propose the planar Abelian dual for SU(N) QCD3 with Ns critical scalars at Chern-
Simons level 2N −Ns−k (k ≥0) shown in Figure 5.2. This duality maps flavor symmetries
to topological symmetries and many baryonic and mesonic operators of one theory are
mapped to gauge invariant disorder operators (monopoles) of its mirror dual.
In this
section, we discuss some simple examples explicitly to justify our proposal. The proposed
duality is believed to hold in the following range of parameters:

The techniques needed to compute the gauge charges of
monopole operators, the dressing needed to construct gauge invariant monopoles and the
spin of the resulting operator are reviewed in Chapter 1.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
As each of the three fermionic fields is quantized in a background of total magnetic flux
1, their spin statistics changes to that of spin-0 bosons9. We conclude that the dressed
monopole operator has Lorentz spin 0, and is the right operator to be mapped to ϕ3 ¯ϕ1.
We can readily identify the off-diagonal mesonic operators and summarize their dual
descriptions—including necessary dressings—in Table 5.1. The mapping of diagonal mesonic
operators |ϕi|2 is less straightforward because these operators are neutral under the max-
imal torus of U(5), visible in the quiver UV Lagrangian. We expect that these operators
are mapped to suitable linear combinations of mesonic operators of the quiver, compatible
with the mass deformations discussed in Section 5.5. We defer the study of the mapping
of diagonal mesonic operators to future work.

This phenomenon is ubiquitous in the quiver theories studied in this paper and the amount

monopole operators with the same global charges but different GNO flux assignment and
dressing, and the mapping will involve a suitable linear combination of these operators. It
would be interesting to analyze this mixing systematically, for example by extending the
techniques of [97, 109, 227] in the presence of monopole interactions, but we defer this to
future work.

Mapping Gauge Invariant Operators
Before we conclude, we briefly comment on the general trend of mapping gauge invariant
operators between the dual theories. We remind the reader that the proposed duality ex-
changes flavor and topological symmetries; hence, mesons, conserved currents and baryons
of the electric theory will be mapped to suitably dressed disorder operators (monopoles)
of the mirror theory. We ask the reader to keep Figure 5.2 in mind for the rest of the
discussion. We also take this opportunity to remind the reader that a specific choice of
fugacities of the topological symmetry parametrizes each column.
Mesons and Conserved Currents
Mesons and conserved currents are Lorentz scalars and vectors, respectively, that trans-
form in the adjoint representation of the flavor symmetry group SU(Ns). We now specify
the general pattern of mapping these operators to monopoles of the mirror theory (Figure
5.2). We focus on the mapping of the 1
2Ns(Ns−1) “off-diagonal” mesons ϕI ¯ϕJ with J < I.
The case J > I can be obtained by taking the Hermitian conjugate of these operators,
which map to monopoles with negative GNO fluxes.
A generic minimal flux mesonic operator cannot be charged under the topological sym-
metry of the first U(1) gauge node in the last row of the quiver diagram. This is due
to the parameterization of topological fugacities and representation-theoretic constraints.
Modulo a subtlety arising from the presence of a monopole interaction in the dual quiver—
already discussed in Section 5.2.1—we propose that off-diagonal mesonic operators are
mapped to monopoles carrying GNO flux under the gauge nodes in the top row and/or
along the rightmost diagonal of the quiver.
To describe this mapping systematically, we find it convenient to organize the mesons
ϕI ¯ϕJ into three distinct classes, based on the values of the flavor indices I and J:
• First, we consider the case where both I and J are less than Ns −N.
• Next, we study the regime where J < Ns −N ≤I.
• Finally, we address the case in which both I and J are greater than or equal to
Ns −N.

Planar Abelianization in CS-QCD3 with Fermions and
Bosons
In this section, we generalize the proposal of the previous section to non-Abelian theories
with fundamental scalars and fermions. We anticipate that adding a fermionic flavor in
the electric theory corresponds to “bosonizing” a column in the planar theory. We provide
several examples of this here.
General Proposal
We propose the following planar, Abelian dual for SU(N) QCD3 with Ns scalars and Nf
fermions at Chern-Simons level 2N −Ns −Nf
2 −k in Figure 5.5. The proposed duality is
believed to hold in the following range of parameters12:
Ns + Nf ≥2N,
Ns ≥2N,
k ≥0
(5.50)
This proposal should be seen as a generalization of the one considered for scalar QCD3 ob-
tained by replacing Nf scalars with Nf fermions and replacing the corresponding fermionic
columns in the planar mirror with purely bosonic columns.
We briefly comment on the potential V shown in Figure 5.5.

Planar Abelian Duals of Unitary CS-QCD3 with Bosons
and Fermions
We have so far focused on constructing duals of SU(N) QCD3 with mixed matter content.
These results, however, extend naturally to the case of unitary QCD3 by gauging the bary-
onic symmetry. From the discussion in Section 5.2, we know that the baryonic symmetry
of the SU(N) QCD3 maps to the topological symmetry of the bottom-left gauge node of
the planar-abelian quiver (Figure 5.2). Gauging of this topological symmetry with zero
CS level introduces a U(1)0 sector with no charged matter whose path integral results in
a delta-function, which effectively ungauges the U(1) bottom-left gauge symmetry. While

Chapter 5
we did not keep track of background CS levels when presenting the duality in Figure
5.2, we expect the duality to involve a non-zero CS level for the baryonic symmetry. In
analogy with the SUSY result of [2, 3], we expect a background CS at level −2 for the
baryonic symmetry on the QCD side of 5.2. Therefore we propose that the dual descrip-
tion for U(N) QCD3 with Ns critical scalars and Nf fermions, where the theory is defined
at Chern-Simons level (2N −Ns −Nf

Consistency Checks via RG Flow Trajectories
In this section, we focus on studying the mapping of mass deformations across dualities,
which serves as an important consistency check for our proposed dualities. We consider
mass deformations for the fundamental fields in the QCD theory and propose a corre-
sponding deformation in the Abelian dual that is partly inspired by the structure of the
supersymmetric dualities of [3, 4] and is a natural generalization of the Abelian case.

Chapter 5
Mass for a Single Field
A simple deformation that can be studied in the QCD3 theory on the LHS of Figure
5.5, consists of giving a mass to a boson or to a fermion that can be either positive or
negative. One could think of it as a linear potential term for a mesonic operator of the
form |ϕ|2 or ¯ψψ. In principle, knowing the map of these operators across the duality 5.5
gives the knowledge of the deformation that is turned on in the dual theory. However,
as we partially comment in Subsection 5.2.4, the map for these operators is not trivial15.
Partially inspired by the SUSY ancestor of the duality, for which the deformations will be
analyzed in [4], we consider examples to discuss massive deformations of the duality 5.5.
An Electric Scalar With A Positive Mass
Concretely, consider SU(2) at CS level 4 −F with F scalars and its planar mirror dual
(top row of Figure 5.11). Suppose we turn on a positive mass term m2|φ3|2 in the electric
QCD3 theory for one of the scalar fields (φ3). We claim that this deformation maps to
real mass terms for the fermions in one of the columns of the dual theory as:
m2|φ3|2
↔
m( ¯ψ1ψ1 −¯ψ2ψ2 + ¯ψ3ψ3) ,
m > 0
(5.70)
where ψ1 and ψ3 are the horizontal fermions in the second column and ψ2 is the diagonal
fermion in the second column. Upon integrating out the massive scalar field, the electric
theory flows to SU(2) QCD3 with F −1 scalars at CS level 4 −F. On the mirror side,
we integrate out the fermions in the second column, leaving behind only BF couplings
between the horizontal gauge nodes, as shown in the bottom row of Figure 5.11. The
resulting duality is exactly the one we propose for SU(N) with F −1 scalars at CS level
2N −k −(F −1), where N = 2 and k = 1 in this case. Notice that this is a natural
generalization of the Abelian case in Figure 5.1, where giving a positive mass to a boson
on the QED side corresponds to giving a positive mass to a fermion in the quiver side. The
choice of signs for the fermionic masses in (5.70) reproduces the BF couplings expected
from the general proposal 5.2.
Immediate generalizations to electric gauge groups with higher rank follow from this ob-
servation. We reiterate that integrating out an electric scalar with a positive mass results
in the integration of a column of fermions in the bulk of its planar mirror, leaving behind
only horizontal BF couplings between gauge nodes.
One can immediately realize that the number of columns composed of fermions in the
bulk of the dual quiver is Ns −2N, where Ns is the number of scalars. Therefore, we can
use this strategy to integrate out only Ns −2N bosons out of Ns. We can not use this
strategy to flow to QCD3 with Ns < 2N, which indeed is beyond the range of validity of
the duality 5.5. In principle, one could study the effect of further massive deformations
to go beyond the range Ns ≥2N, this is a quite non-trivial exercise and we leave it for
future work. However, we propose examples of dualities with Ns < 2N in Section 5.6.
One could also consider a negative mass for an electric scalar, which would condense and
Higgs the gauge group SU(N)2N−Ns−k →SU(N −1)2(N−1)−(Ns−1)−k′, where k′ = k −1.
We stress that the map of the deformation in (5.70) works only for m > 0. It would be
interesting to see how this may be seen in the planar mirror theory, but it is beyond the
scope of this work
15We recall that the reason behind the difficulty of this map is that these operators are not charged
under the Cartan subgroup of the global symmetry.

However, we propose examples of dualities with Ns < 2N in Section 5.6.
One could also consider a negative mass for an electric scalar, which would condense and
Higgs the gauge group SU(N)2N−Ns−k →SU(N −1)2(N−1)−(Ns−1)−k′, where k′ = k −1.
We stress that the map of the deformation in (5.70) works only for m > 0. It would be

15We recall that the reason behind the difficulty of this map is that these operators are not charged
under the Cartan subgroup of the global symmetry. Indeed, in the dual theory, there are a large number
of operators, carrying the correct spin, that are not charged under the global symmetry, and in principle
any linear combination of them is a valid candidate as the target of the map.

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
Immediate generalizations to electric gauge groups with higher rank follow from this obser-

We conclude by mentioning that in the duality 5.5, each fermion in the QCD3 corresponds
to a column of bosons in the dual theory. Therefore, the results summarized in Figure
5.12 and 5.13 can be used to study the effect on the dual theory of a mass for any number
of fermions in the QCD3.
Duals of Topological Quantum Field Theories
Instead of studying mass deformations of individual bosonic or fermionic fields, one may
consider turning on masses for all matter fields simultaneously. In particular, we can give
positive masses to all scalars and a homogeneous mass to all fermions in the QCD3 theory.
Such deformations generically gap out the matter sector and drive the theory to a purely
topological infrared phase, described by a TQFT.
Since the original non-supersymmetric duality relates two theories that are expected to
flow to the same IR fixed point, the corresponding mass-deformed theories should also
flow to dual TQFTs. However, a direct analysis of these flows in the non-supersymmetric
setting rapidly becomes complicated. A more tractable strategy is therefore to exploit
the existence of supersymmetric “ancestor” dualities: one can study gapped phases of the
corresponding N = 2 theories, where the renormalization group flows are better controlled,
and then infer the resulting TQFT dualities. We expect that the TQFTs obtained in this
way coincide with those arising directly from mass-deforming the non-supersymmetric
dual pairs.
A detailed analysis of these supersymmetric flows is presented in [5]. Here, we focus on
the outcome in two representative cases, corresponding to SQCD theories whose Abelian
planar duals contain either one or zero “bulk” columns.
The case of SU(N)−1 with 2N scalars
We first consider the duality for SU(N)−1 QCD with Ns = 2N scalars and no fermions,
shown in Figure 5.2. Assigning positive masses to all scalars gaps the matter fields and
leaves behind a pure SU(N)−1 Chern-Simons theory in the infrared.
On the Abelian side, the planar quiver dual contains a single empty bulk column (see
Figure 5.2). This structure plays a crucial role in determining the resulting topological
theory. More generally, the analysis described here applies to any QCD theory whose
Abelian dual contains exactly one bulk column.
Rather than following the non-supersymmetric flow directly, we consider a chiral-planar
N = 2 duality studied in [3], in which the electric theory flows to the same SU(N)−1 TQFT
after appropriate mass deformations. In the supersymmetric setting, these deformations
can be explicitly tracked on the planar Abelian side [4], leading to an Abelian Chern-
Simons theory with gauge group U(1)2 and Chern–Simons matrix

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
The case of SU(N)0 with 2N scalars
A similar analysis applies to SU(N)0 QCD with Ns = 2N scalars. Giving positive masses
to all scalars drives the theory to pure SU(N)0 Yang–Mills in the infrared. The corre-
sponding supersymmetric analysis, together with its planar Abelian dual, predicts that
the dual theory flows to a U(1)1 Chern–Simons theory. This is an almost trivial TQFT,
in agreement with the expected infrared behavior of the electric theory.
Probing the Ns < 2N Regime
In this section, we attempt to study theories where the number of scalars Ns is less than
2N. We find that we are able to consistently bosonize two columns of height N −1 outside
of the bulk of the quiver gauge theory, which means that in the duality (5.5) we replace the
tallest column in the two triangular sections with a column made only by bosons. Each
replacement of columns using this strategy consists of decreasing/increasing the number
of bosons/fermions by 1 in duality (5.5). We emphasize that this can be done to reduce
the number of bosons by 2 at most.
This strategy allows us to propose a quiver which is dual to a QCD3 theory with Ns =
2N −1 or Ns = 2N −2 scalars and Nf fermions such that Ns +Nf ≥2N, generalizing the
dualities discussed in Section 5.3. Extending this procedure to even lower Ns by consider-
ing quivers with more bosonic columns in the outer triangle would require a generalization
of our prescription to construct monopole interactions. We leave this to future work.
We illustrate this proposal using two examples, one with Ns = 2N −1 and one with
Ns = 2N −2. In particular, we notice that the SU(2) QCD3 when Ns = 2 and Nf ≥2 is
dual to a completely bosonic planar theory.
An Example: SU(3)0 QCD3 with Ns = 5 Scalars and Nf = 2 Fermions
We consider the example of SU(3)0 with 5 scalars and 2 fermions, and its planar dual
description. We propose the duality shown in Figure 5.15. Notice that the second-to-last
column has been “bosonized” in the sense that it is made only of scalar fields, similar to
the column in the bulk.

Scope and Future Directions
We restrict ourselves to the study of SU(N) QCD3 with at least Ns = 2N −2 bosonic
fundamental flavors and any number of fermionic fundamental flavors.
Extending our
proposal to study duals for smaller values of Ns would be the next step. This can be
achieved by further mass deformations of the present dualities, as discussed in [5] for the
N = 2 supersymmetric case.
Another promising direction of research would be to develop an algorithmic paradigm
to construct these bosonization dualities, in a vein similar to their N = 2 [3, 26] and
N = 4 [188] counterparts. Such an approach would help construct planar Abelian duals
for broader classes of non-Abelian gauge theories.
Part V: Conclusions and Outlook

Chapter 6
Conclusions and Outlook
Mirror symmetry for N = 4 theories admits a compelling and intuitive interpretation in
terms of Type IIB string theory and Hanany–Witten brane constructions. In this setting,
the exchange of Higgs and Coulomb branches, as well as the corresponding interchange
of flavor and topological symmetries, is geometrically manifest. However, this intuition
ceases to apply once the amount of supersymmetry is reduced. For theories with N < 4,
the Higgs and Coulomb branches may be lifted or collapse to isolated vacua, and the
canonical brane realizations underlying mirror symmetry are often absent. As a result,
geometric engineering alone is no longer a reliable guide.
The primary goal of this thesis has been to investigate whether mirror-like dualities can
nevertheless persist in theories with N = 2, and even in the absence of supersymme-
try. Despite the breakdown of string-theoretic intuition, symmetry considerations remain
sufficiently powerful to uncover and test such dualities. In particular, we find that the
defining structural feature of mirror symmetry — namely, the exchange of topological and
flavor symmetries — continues to provide a robust organizing principle beyond the N = 4
setting.
From this perspective, mirror symmetry should be viewed more generally as a duality
that maps perturbative, order-type operators to non-perturbative, disorder-type operators,
such as monopole operators, rather than as a consequence of a specific brane construction.
This viewpoint allows mirror symmetry to be formulated and tested intrinsically within
quantum field theory, opening the door to systematic explorations of dualities in theories
with reduced, or no, supersymmetry.
A central result of this thesis is the development of the paradigm of Planar Abelianiza-
tion, which proves especially compelling for several reasons:
• The resulting N = 2 mirror duals are Abelian and admit a Lagrangian description
— two highly nontrivial and a priori independent properties.
• The construction admits straightforward generalizations to AN and DN quivers, the-
ories with symplectic and special unitary gauge groups, circular quivers, and models
with rich matter content, including adjoint fields, rank-2 antisymmetric tensors, as
well as the presence of both fundamental and anti-fundamental representations [3,
6].
• The framework enables systematic deformations, yielding theories with more gen-
eral Chern–Simons levels and flavor content [5]. For special choices of parameters,

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
the resulting theories display a rich infrared phenomenology, such as s-confinement,
chiral symmetry breaking, and the emergence of non-Abelian TQFTs.
• The paradigm further admits extensions beyond supersymmetry, encompassing non-
supersymmetric dualities [4].
In addition to the future directions discussed at the end of each chapter, we now briefly
outline several further avenues of investigation suggested by the results of this thesis. 1
The Road to Orthosymplectic Mirror Symmetry
Mirror duals of N = 4 orthogonal and symplectic SQCD can be engineered using Feng-
Hanany brane setups [207], which incorporate orientifold planes in addition to the standard
Hanany–Witten configurations. These constructions typically give rise to quivers with
alternating orthogonal and symplectic gauge groups.
Even in the presence of N = 4
supersymmetry, analyzing these theories remains highly nontrivial. This complexity arises
from the emergence of various symmetries acting on the moduli space in the deep infrared,
which are absent in the UV Lagrangian and hence invisible to supersymmetric localization.
A number of tools, such as the magnetic quivers paradigm, have been developed to study
these theories [208, 209, 229, 230], but a complete and detailed analysis remains intricate.
Both N = 4 U(N) and USp(2N) SQCD with F hypermultiplets admit RG flows under
supersymmetry-breaking deformations to N = 2 vacua in which the gauge symmetries are
Higgsed to their maximal tori, U(1)N. Finding these vacua is nontrivial, but they form the
building blocks of algorithmic planar dual constructions, which proceed by gluing together
“planarized” U(N) [3] or USp(2N) [214] blocks.
A natural question is whether SO(2N) and SO(2N + 1) SQCD with F hypermultiplets
also admit such flows. The answer is affirmative: these theories likewise reach N = 2
vacua in which the gauge symmetries are Higgsed to U(1)N, as illustrated in Equation
6.1. This observation is important because, even if a fully planar Abelianization of or-
thosymplectic quivers is not yet known, the existence of these planar limits suggests that
a proto-algorithm for constructing orthosymplectic planar duals may still be feasible.

Towards non-Abelian 2d N = (2, 2) Mirrors
Mirror symmetry also admits a rich realization in two dimensions.
In contrast to its
three-dimensional avatar [175, 231], two-dimensional mirror symmetry does not involve
monopole operators or topological symmetries.
Instead, it relates seemingly different
descriptions of N = (2, 2) supersymmetric quantum field theories. In its simplest form, it
maps a non-linear sigma model with a Kähler target space to a Landau–Ginzburg theory
with a superpotential, exchanging geometric data with purely holomorphic information [94,
95, 232–235]. From a physical perspective, this duality exchanges momentum and winding
modes and interchanges complex-structure and Kähler moduli, providing a powerful non-
perturbative equivalence between theories with very different ultraviolet descriptions.
It is natural to expect a deeper connection between three-dimensional and two-dimensional
mirror symmetry [32].
However, the reduction of three-dimensional dualities to two-
dimensional ones is a delicate matter [236], for several reasons.
• Supersymmetric theories with four supercharges often possess a classical moduli
space of vacua (at least for suitable values of their parameters), and two-dimensional
theories are no exception. This moduli space can be modified or lifted by quantum
corrections. In higher dimensions, each point on the quantum moduli space cor-
responds to a distinct vacuum lying in a different superselection sector, and the

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
low-energy theory at a generic point is free, while interacting dynamics arise only
near singular loci. In contrast, in two dimensions quantum fluctuations are strong
enough that the ground state (and, more generally, all states) explore the entire
target space or “moduli space.”
• The sigma-model metric is classically marginal in two dimensions and is generically
renormalized. As a result, it cannot be neglected in the analysis of infrared dynamics.
This issue is particularly acute for non-compact target spaces or in the presence of
runaway directions, where supersymmetric configurations may lie at infinite distance.
In such cases, there may be no normalizable ground state, and instead a continuum
of states can extend down to zero energy, depending on the asymptotic behavior of
the Kähler potential. Such continua can contribute to supersymmetric indices in
ways that differ qualitatively from the contributions of isolated vacua.
• A phenomenon unique to two dimensions is the possibility that a single ultraviolet
supersymmetric theory can flow to more than one superconformal field theory in the
infrared. More precisely, the infrared limit may be a direct sum of decoupled SCFTs,
so that the Hilbert space is a sum rather than a tensor product of the individual
Hilbert spaces.
These features become particularly significant when compactifying three-dimensional N =
2 theories on a circle. In this context, different ways of taking the radius of the circle to zero
can lead to distinct two-dimensional conformal field theories lying on the same conformal
manifold.
Constructing mirror duals of two-dimensional N = (2, 2) theories thus remains a formidable
challenge. A variety of complementary approaches have been developed, including anal-
yses based on A- and B-twists, constraints from K-theory [237–240], and the study of
topologically twisted indices [127, 241].
The approach we advocate builds on the results of [242–244].
Upon supersymmetric
localization of three-dimensional theories on S2 × S1 or D2 × S1, the resulting partition
functions admit distinct asymptotic limits as the radius of S1 is taken to zero. The precise
infrared limit depends on how real mass parameters and Fayet–Iliopoulos terms are scaled
in this process.
Concretely, two inequivalent limits can be defined:
Higgs limit:

In the Higgs limit, matter fields remain light and the effective theory localizes onto the
Higgs branch. The resulting partition function reduces to that of a two-dimensional theory
on S2 [245–248] or on D2 [249–251], depending on the geometry.
By contrast, in the Coulomb limit all matter fields become heavy and are integrated out.
The resulting expression can be identified with a Dotsenko–Fateev conformal block [252–
256], and, with some significant mathematical gymnastics, can be recast into the twisted
superpotential of a Landau-Ginzburg model with a logarithmic twisted superpotential.
For a pair of three-dimensional mirror dual theories, the two limits described above are not
independent. Mirror symmetry exchanges the Higgs and Coulomb branches, and therefore

Chapter 6
taking the Coulomb limit of one theory necessarily forces the Higgs limit of its mirror dual,
and vice versa. As a result, mirror symmetry in three dimensions descends to a nontrivial
equivalence between distinct two-dimensional limits of the same parent theory.
It is therefore natural to apply this framework to the chiral–planar mirror dualities studied
in this work. By taking the Coulomb limit on one side of the duality and the Higgs limit
on the other, one obtains pairs of N = (2, 2) partition functions which are expected
to coincide. Studying these limits provides a concrete and quantitative probe of mirror
symmetry in two dimensions and offers a promising route toward identifying and testing
mirror duals of non-Abelian N = (2, 2) theories.2
Non-Abelian (p, q)-Webs
Brane configurations for five-dimensional N = 1 gauge theories were first constructed in
[158], following the general framework pioneered in [19], using Type IIB D5- and NS5-
branes. In Type IIB string theory, fivebranes transform as a (p, q) multiplet under the
SL(2; Z) duality group, where the D5-brane corresponds to a (0, 1) fivebrane and the
NS5-brane to a (1, 0) fivebrane.
At the classical level, a configuration in which a D5-brane ends on an NS5-brane preserves
eight supercharges. Quantum mechanically, however, this picture is modified: the NS5-
brane bends due to a Coulomb-like interaction sourced by the attached D5-brane [154,
263, 264]. A more accurate description is that charge conservation forbids a pure D5-brane
from ending on a pure NS5-brane. Instead, at the junction the two combine into a bound
state carrying both charges, known as a (1, 1) fivebrane.
More generally, bound states of (p, q) fivebranes can be formed whenever the (p, q) charges
are conserved. The resulting webs of (p, q) fivebranes provide a powerful geometric frame-
work for engineering five-dimensional N = 1 gauge theories. Analyzing the interacting
five-dimensional worldvolume theories supported on these configurations is an interesting
and formidable problem, which we will not mention beyond this.
(p, q) fivebranes also play an important role in the geometric engineering of three-dimensional
gauge theories, where they naturally give rise to Chern–Simons interactions. From this
perspective, it is natural to expect that the chiral–planar mirror duals studied in this
thesis may admit a Type IIB realization in terms of brane tilings constructed from (p, q)
fivebranes. Such a description would provide a geometric underpinning for these dualities
and merits a renewed and systematic investigation of their properties.
[265] studied the low-energy effective theory on the worldvolume of two D3-branes ending
a (1, K)-web on each side. Such N = 2 theories display a non-trivial tetrality, shown in
Equation 6.2, where we have suppressed the details of the superpotential for brevity.
2These problems are interesting mathematical questions in their own right and may provide insight into
toric fibrations over Grassmannian manifolds and into the interplay between branes, mirror symmetry, and
toric geometry [257–262].

The N = 2 SL(2; Z) Algorithm
We remarked earlier that Type IIB string theory enjoys an SL(2; Z) duality symmetry,
and that mirror symmetry for N = 4 theories admits a natural interpretation in terms
of the action of the S generator.
For N = 4 gauge theories, the S transformation is
implemented by coupling to the FT[U(N)] theory.
The T generator of SL(2; Z), on the other hand, corresponds to a shift of the Chern–
Simons level of a background gauge field [87]. As in the case of S, the action of T admits
a local interpretation on brane configurations.
Building on this observation, a systematic N = 4 dualization algorithm was introduced in
[188], in which general duality transformations are constructed as sequences of S and T
operations acting locally on brane configurations. This framework reveals a large web of
three-dimensional dualities unified by the action of a single SL(2; Z) structure.
We have already encountered the action of the S generator in the context of N = 2 theories
in this thesis. It is therefore natural to ask whether the paradigm of planar Abelianization

The ABCD’s of Mirror Symmetry
can be extended to construct N = 2 analogues of the T generator. Pursuing this direction
suggests the existence of an N = 2 realization of the SL(2; Z) dualization algorithm. As
in the N = 4 case, such a framework is expected to organize and generate large classes of
dualities among three-dimensional theories with reduced supersymmetry and connect our
N = 2 chiral-planar mirror duals to other existing constructions [26, 158, 178, 266–268].
Part VI: Appendices

Appendix A
Higher Dimensional Constructions
Many of the structures and dualities encountered in lower-dimensional supersymmetric
gauge theories admit a unifying interpretation when viewed from a higher-dimensional
perspective. In particular, a large class of four- and three-dimensional supersymmetric
quantum field theories can be obtained by compactifying the six-dimensional N = (2, 0)
superconformal field theory on suitably chosen manifolds. This viewpoint underlies the
construction of Class S theories, as well as the AGT correspondence relating supersym-
metric gauge theories to two-dimensional conformal field theories.
The purpose of this appendix is to provide a brief and self-contained review of these ideas,
with an emphasis on their conceptual role rather than on technical details. We summarize
the essential features of the six-dimensional N = (2, 0) theory, its compactification on
Riemann surfaces leading to Class S constructions, and the basic statement of the AGT
correspondence. This discussion is intended solely to place the results of the main text
in a broader theoretical context, and we will not attempt an exhaustive or pedagogical
treatment of these subjects.
A.1
SCFTs in Six Dimensions
Six-dimensional superconformal field theories play a distinguished role in the landscape
of supersymmetric quantum field theories, often serving as master theories from which
many lower-dimensional constructions descend. This privileged status stems from the fact
that superconformal algebras do not exist in spacetime dimensions D > 6 [269]. The
obstruction arises from the additional constraints imposed by superconformality on Lie
superalgebras: the bosonic subalgebra g0 must act on the fermionic generators g1 in a
spinorial representation [69]. Remarkably, this requirement is satisfied only marginally in
six dimensions. The triality properties of the complexified conformal group SO(6, 2; C)
allow vector representations of g0 to be interchanged with spinorial representations of g1,
thereby permitting the existence of superconformal symmetry. No such construction is
possible in higher dimensions, rendering six dimensions the upper critical dimension for
interacting SCFTs.
The on-shell representations of six-dimensional supersymmetry are well known and consist
of the following multiplets [270, 271]:
• the hypermultiplet, containing four real scalars and two spin- 1
2 fermions;
• the vector multiplet, containing a vector field and two gaugini;

• the tensor multiplet, containing a real scalar, a spin- 1
2 fermion, and a two-form
gauge field with anti-self-dual field strength;
• the gravity multiplet, containing the graviton, two gravitini, and a chiral two-form
gauge field with self-dual field strength.
The requirement that the theory admit a conserved stress-energy tensor places strong
restrictions on the allowed amount of supersymmetry. In six dimensions, this implies that
interacting superconformal theories can have at most sixteen supercharges, corresponding
to N = (1, 0), N = (2, 0), or N = (1, 1) supersymmetry.
Six-dimensional SCFTs are intrinsically non-gravitational theories.
As such, they are
built from combinations of hypermultiplets, vector multiplets, and tensor multiplets, but
do not include dynamical gravity. A crucial and somewhat counterintuitive feature of
these theories is that, unlike in lower dimensions, interacting conformal field theories in
six dimensions do not generally admit a Lorentz-covariant Lagrangian description. Indeed,
only trivial examples—such as the free scalar theory
L = 1
2(∂φ)2
—possess a conventional local action.
In particular, N = (2, 0) superconformal theories necessarily contain tensor multiplets,
whose anti-self-dual two-form fields obstruct the existence of a manifestly Lorentz-covariant
action.
As a result, these theories are best understood through indirect means, such
as their operator content, symmetry structure, and protected observables, rather than
through a microscopic Lagrangian formulation.
This conceptual impasse was resolved during the Second String Revolution, which revealed
a geometric and string-theoretic origin for six-dimensional SCFTs [272]. The key insight is
that such theories can arise as decoupling limits of string or M-theory compactifications:
by compactifying extra dimensions, decoupling gravity, and tuning parameters to special
loci in moduli space, one encounters regimes in which effective strings become tensionless.
The resulting interacting fixed points furnish precisely the six-dimensional superconformal
field theories that lack a conventional Lagrangian description.
A remarkable property of six-dimensional N = (2, 0) superconformal field theories is that
interacting theories are classified by simply-laced Lie algebras of ADE type. That is, there
exists a family of N = (2, 0) SCFTs labeled by g = AN−1, DN, E6, E7, and E8, with no
known interacting theories outside this classification.
This ADE classification admits a natural geometric interpretation in string and M-theory.
N = (2, 0) theories of type g arise as the low-energy worldvolume theories of coincident M5-
branes, or equivalently from Type IIB string theory compactified on ALE spaces with ADE
singularities. In this picture, the appearance of ADE algebras is tied to the classification
of isolated C2/ΓADE singularities, and the tensionless strings of the six-dimensional theory
originate from M2-branes wrapping vanishing two-cycles at the singular locus.
Although six-dimensional N = (2, 0) SCFTs do not admit a conventional Lagrangian de-
scription, their physical content becomes accessible upon compactification. Compactifying
these theories on a circle produces maximally supersymmetric five-dimensional Yang–Mills
theories, while compactification on lower-dimensional manifolds gives rise to a rich web of
supersymmetric quantum field theories in four and three dimensions. In particular, many
dualities and non-perturbative phenomena observed in lower-dimensional supersymmetric
gauge theories can be understood as descending from geometric operations—such as degen-

erations or dualities of the compactification manifold—at the level of the six-dimensional
parent theory.
For this reason, six-dimensional SCFTs provide a unifying framework for a wide class of
supersymmetric field theories and dualities.
A.2
Class S Constructions
As discussed above, interacting six-dimensional superconformal field theories do not admit
a conventional Lagrangian description. Nevertheless, a powerful handle on their dynamics
is obtained by compactifying them on lower-dimensional manifolds. In particular, one
may consider compactifying a six-dimensional N = (2, 0) SCFT on a Riemann surface C,
performing a partial topological twist to preserve supersymmetry. The resulting lower-
dimensional theories inherit much of their structure from the geometry of C and from the
choice of defects inserted along it.
The four-dimensional N = 2 theories obtained in this way are known as Class S theo-
ries1. These constructions provide a geometric framework for understanding a vast class of
supersymmetric quantum field theories, encoding their matter content, couplings, and du-
alities in terms of the topology and complex structure of the underlying Riemann surface
[213, 273, 274].
Seiberg-Witten Geometry.
As a motivating example, we focus on Seiberg–Witten
geometry in four-dimensional N = 2 gauge theories [275]. From a purely four-dimensional
perspective, the appearance of the Seiberg–Witten curve and its relation to the low-energy
prepotential may seem somewhat ad hoc.
This structure, however, becomes much more natural when viewed through the lens of
Class S constructions. In this framework, the Seiberg–Witten curve is not an auxiliary
object introduced to solve the theory, but rather has a direct geometric origin: it is
precisely the Riemann surface on which the parent six-dimensional N = (2, 0) theory is
compactified. The Seiberg–Witten geometry of the four-dimensional theory is therefore
inherited directly from the compactification geometry of the underlying six-dimensional
SCFT.
The AGT Correspondence.
Another important duality uncovered using these con-
structions is the 4d/2d-correspondence, or the Alday-Gaiotto-Tachikawa (AGT) correspon-
dence [134, 276, 277].
The basic idea can be summarized as follows. One starts from a six-dimensional SCFT
χ(g) and considers its partition function Z on a product manifold of the form W4 × C2,
where W4 is a four-dimensional manifold and C2 is a two-dimensional Riemann surface.
The resulting quantity Z[W4 × C2] depends in a controlled way on the geometric and
topological properties of both factors.
Crucially, the same partition function can be evaluated in two equivalent ways, depending
on which factor is regarded as the compactification manifold:
Z[W4 × C2] ≡Z[W4]
C2 = Z[C2]
W4 .
That is, one may either interpret the result as a two-dimensional theory living on C2,
obtained by compactifying the six-dimensional theory on W4, or as a four-dimensional
1The notation S, much to the author’s disappointment, stands for “six”.

theory on W4, obtained by compactification on C2. In terms of observables, the AGT
correspondence relates 4d partition functions to 2d conformal blocks.
The 3d/3d Correspondence.
A closely related example of this philosophy is the 3d/3d
correspondence, which relates supersymmetric quantum field theories in three dimensions
to geometric and topological data of three-manifolds [278, 279]. Starting from the six-
dimensional N = (2, 0) theory of type g, one considers its compactification on a three-
manifold M3, possibly with a partial topological twist. This construction gives rise to
a three-dimensional N = 2 theory, often denoted T[M3], whose protected observables
capture invariants of M3.
As in the AGT correspondence, the same six-dimensional partition function may be evalu-
ated by exchanging the roles of spacetime and internal geometry. In particular, supersym-
metric partition functions of T[M3] on curved three-manifolds—such as S3
b or S2 ×S1—are
related to topological invariants of M3, including Chern–Simons theory and quantum in-
variants of knots and three-manifolds. The 3d/3d correspondence thus provides a powerful
bridge between three-dimensional supersymmetric field theories, low-dimensional topology,
and quantum geometry.
It would be interesting to study the chiral-planar dualities presented in this thesis with the
machinery of Class S. While it seems possible to recover 2d N = (2, 2) mirror symmetry
by compactifying the chiral-planar mirror pairs on S1, it may also be possible to study the
holomorphic blocks of the resulting theory to arrive at the putative partition function of
a 4d N = 1 theory (a la [280]). In this way, we may realise a rich web of dualities across
dimensions.

Appendix B
From the basic N = 4 Abelian
confining dualities to the N = 2
Abelian dualities
In this appendix, we show how to prove the self-dualities for U(1)0 with [2, 2] flavors, for
U(1)1/2 with [2, 1] flavors and U(1)1 with [1, 1] flavors assuming only that U(1)0 with [1, 1]
flavors confines.
Starting from the basic N = 4 Abelian mirror duality, we present the Aharony-like duali-
ties needed to dualize the planar quivers presented in the paper. We start from the basic
N = 4 Abelian mirror dual pair (Equation B.1) which relates a U(1) gauge theory with
one hypermultiplet to a free hypermultiplet.

Self-Dual SQED
By composing the duality operation shown in Equation B.1, one can derive mirror dual
pairs of SQED with a generic number of hypermultiplets a la Kapustin-Strassler [23]. We
provide a brief summary of the piecewise dualization procedure needed to find the mirror
of N = 2 SQED with F flavors [265]. Each flavor is dualized using Equation B.3 to obtain
a U(1)F+1 with 2F charged fields, F flippers (and a corresponding superpotential) and 2F
mixed BF interactions connecting the original U(1) gauge group with the 2F new gauge
factors. At this point, the original U(1) gauge group has no flavors and we can perform
the functional integral over it obtaining a functional delta function. Implementing the
delta reduces the number of gauge groups to F −1, which is indeed the correct mirror
dual.
To dualize the planar quivers in this paper, we need some additional self-dualities of N = 2
SQED with F = 2. The starting point of this is the N = 2 self-dual (modulo flips) pair
shown in Equation B.5:

B.1
An Application: Recovering the Symmetric Bifunda-
mental Block from a Planar Abelian Quiver Gauge The-
ory
In Equation 2.79, it was demonstrated that N free chiral fields are mirror dual to a planar
quiver gauge theory. This is schematically repeated in Figure B.1.

Figure B.1: The duality between the [N, N] bifundamental block and a planar quiver
gauge theory is shown here.
Our aim in this section is to sequentially confine the planar quivers using only the pairs
of Aharony duals discussed before.
We demonstrate how to reduce the planar mirror of an [N, N] bifundamental block to
the planar mirror of an [N −1, N −1] bifundamental block by following a sequence of
columnwise Aharony dualizations here. Performing this sequence iteratively will confine
the planar mirror.

Thus, we correctly recover all 9 components of the [3, 3] bifundamental block.
Thus, we observe that the planar mirror of an [N, N] bifundamental block can be system-

atically confined to yield the planar mirror of an [N−1, N−1] bifundamental block. This
is achieved by sequentially dualizing each column of the planar quiver—first from left to
right, and then again from right to left.
